6649: Archaeology and Cultural Evolution
6650: 
6651: In this chapter I want to look at the way different theoretical
6652: approaches treat the question of cultural evolution. As with the
6653: archaeology of gender, we shall find that different attitudes to
6654: evolution tend to betray different attitudes to archaeological theory
6655: and practice as a whole. Again, we shall also find that different
6656: theoretical attitudes within archaeology are paralleled by a similar
6657: range of attitudes within the human sciences as a whole.
6658: 
6659: As with other terms, the word ‘evolution’ is not easily defined.
6660: Evolution has come to have an array of different meanings, some at odds
6661: with or even completely contradicting others. The best way to explain
6662: what these different meanings are is to look at the history of
6663: archaeology and of evolution. The origins of evolutionary ideas can be
6664: traced back to the Renaissance of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries,
6665: and in particular to the first encounters between Europeans and other
6666: peoples around the world. If we consider these origins, we find that
6667: early evolutionary ideas were closely bound up with the development of
6668: archaeology as a discipline, to the extent that it is difficult to
6669: conceive of one without the other.
6670: 
6671: Before the fifteenth century in Europe, there was a consciousness of a
6672: historical past. Texts, most obviously the Bible, gave a range of dates
6673: for events and occurrences that stretched back to the creation of the
6674: world (famously glossed by Archbishop Ussher as the night preceding 23
6675: October 4004 BC). There was also an emphasis in the medieval world on
6676: the importance of custom and tradition, ideas which served to articulate
6677: past and present and to provide a justification for practices and
6678: community identities in a world where most ordinary folk were
6679: illiterate. However, it is possible to argue that there was little or no
6680: conception of the past as fundamentally different from the present.
6681: People in the past were considered to be ‘just like us’; look at any
6682: portrayal of St George, a Roman soldier, in a medieval stained glass
6683: window, where he is given medieval armour and weaponry (figure 9.1).
6684: Roman emperors were described in terms that made them sound like
6685: medieval princes. The idea that past peoples practised different ways of
6686: life, had different cultures and beliefs, and that these needed to be
6687: studied on their own terms was, therefore, problematic before the
6688: Renaissance.
6689: 
6690: Figure 9.1 St George and the Dragon, as depicted on St George’s
6691: Altarpiece, National Gallery, Prague, c.1470. from Bohemian Art of the
6692: Gothic and Early Renaissance Periods, Press Foto, Praha,
6693: http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:St_George_and_the_Dragon-altar_wing-NG-Praha.jpg
6694: 
6695: [c09_image001.jpg]
6696: 
6697: Fifteenth-and sixteenth-century European explorers and colonists faced
6698: an intellectual paradox when they encountered peoples in other areas of
6699: the globe, in particular Native Americans. Native peoples were clearly
6700: different. This difference posed a very basic intellectual challenge to
6701: Renaissance intellectuals, a challenge articulated for example by
6702: Shakespeare’s play The Tempest, in which the encounter and struggle for
6703: power between the Milanese magician and book-lover Prospero and the
6704: native ‘monster’ Caliban is placed literally at centre stage. In the
6705: view of many European thinkers, here in the forests of what the poet
6706: John Donne called the ‘newfound land’ of America were human beings who
6707: practised none of the civilized arts – they went naked, had no formal
6708: code of law, no state, and above all, no (Christian) religion. And yet
6709: such ‘savages’ could act in noble and civilized ways. This was difficult
6710: to explain within the sixteenth-century European scheme of things to say
6711: the least.
6712: 
6713: One way out of the intellectual conundrum was to suggest a powerful
6714: idea: perhaps ‘we’, the civilized peoples of Europe, were like this in
6715: the past. Perhaps, in other words, such ‘savages’ represented some sort
6716: of earlier or lower order of human existence from which Renaissance
6717: ‘Man’ had evolved. Hence, for example, the portrayals by early
6718: antiquarians of ‘early Britons’ were often closely modelled on those of
6719: Native Americans (figures 9.2 and 9.3).
6720: 
6721: At the same time as Europeans were exploring other parts of the globe,
6722: early antiquarians were exploring historic and prehistoric monuments in
6723: the landscape around them in Europe. As a result of the colonial
6724: encounter and the intellectual challenge it posed to European thinkers,
6725: early antiquarians began to think about a distant prehistoric past while
6726: simultaneously they began to explore its antiquities through
6727: barrow-digging and surveying of ancient monuments. From reflection on
6728: these discoveries emerged the idea that is central to many versions of
6729: cultural or social evolution, namely that different peoples in different
6730: areas of the world went through similar sorts of social changes and
6731: processes. At its most simple, these social changes and processes were
6732: conceived of as a single, fundamental transition that all societies went
6733: through, between ‘savagery’ and ‘civilization’, ‘them’ and ‘us’.
6734: 
6735: In its earlier forms, evolutionary thought was presented as an abstract
6736: story. The seventeenth-century English philosopher Thomas Hobbes for
6737: example talked about how, before the emergence of a central power, the
6738: life of humans was ‘nasty, brutish and short’, there being no rule of
6739: law to ameliorate a general anarchy, with every man for himself. Hobbes
6740: related how, therefore, men’s independence was given up to a
6741: ‘commonwealth’ or a State power over everyone. Hobbes’ story was never
6742: intended as a factual account of the past; its intended focus and
6743: message was largely about the political events of Hobbes’ times. Hobbes
6744: sought to justify and legitimate political obedience and the rule of law
6745: in a very turbulent seventeenthcentury world, in which a
6746: divinely-appointed King Charles had just been executed in the name of
6747: the Commonwealth, and in which therefore divine legitimation for
6748: obedience to the state had been abruptly and violently removed.
6749: 
6750: Figure 9.2 A Native American chief, drawn by John White in the 1580s
6751: 
6752: [c09_image002.jpg]
6753: 
6754: Figure 9.3 An ‘Ancient Briton’, drawn by John White in the 1580s
6755: 
6756: [c09_image003.jpg]
6757: 
6758: It is possible to trace Hobbes’ abstracted account of evolution through
6759: later thinkers, for example Rousseau’s social contract, and more
6760: generally the rise of the idea of progress in human affairs; however,
6761: for the purposes of this chapter, we will move to a critical moment in
6762: the middle of the nineteenth century.
6763: 
6764: Darwin, Marx and Spencer
6765: 
6766: A number of intellectual strands came together in the middle of the
6767: nineteenth century. First, the colonial expansion of Europe across the
6768: globe went along with an ethos of ‘discovery’. For the natural world,
6769: scholars mapped and classified different species (though it is worth
6770: noting that both prior to and including Darwin, observation of the local
6771: landscape such as that of southern England was equally important to
6772: their findings). Intellectuals also mapped the different human cultures
6773: to be found across the world, and needed a ordered and coherent system
6774: in order to classify what they had found.
6775: 
6776: One such scheme was that of Lewis Henry Morgan, published in 1877 as
6777: Ancient Society. Morgan drew on the earlier work of Tylor, and
6778: classified different social forms and institutions such as subsistence
6779: practices, family and kinship forms, language and religion. His ordering
6780: scheme was in terms of human progress, with ‘savagery’ being followed by
6781: ‘barbarism’ and then ‘civilization’.
6782: 
6783: Morgan’s work was drawn on heavily by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels. We
6784: saw in chapter 7 how Marx’s scheme of human history could be classified
6785: as evolutionary, in its delineation of a succession of ever more complex
6786: social forms from ‘primitive communism’ upwards to capitalism. In The
6787: Origins of the Family, Private Property and the State, Engels argued
6788: that the modern institutions of marriage and properly relations were not
6789: ‘normal’, but rather were specific historical creations, which came into
6790: being alongside State society. The evidence for Marx and Engels’
6791: arguments, and before them those of Morgan, was arguably flawed, but the
6792: implications of their arguments that, for example household and gender
6793: relations were intimately tied to the State went on to be highly
6794: influential on feminist thinking. This body of work also influenced
6795: Gordon Childe, who in Man Makes Himself and What Happened in History
6796: adopted Maine’s savagery-barbarism-civilization scheme and attempted to
6797: link it to the three-age system of Stone, Bronze and Iron Ages.
6798: 
6799: Though he did not deal primarily with archaeology, Herbert Spencer’s
6800: work was especially important in that he elaborated and popularized the
6801: idea that all human societies move from a less to a more complex state
6802: of organization. He further linked this idea to notions of morality and
6803: human ‘progress’, and set the tone for a generation of self-confident
6804: Victorian intellectuals. In Spencer’s thought, ‘civilized’ society was
6805: not only a historical development: it was more moral than ‘savagery’. It
6806: is partly as a consequence of Spencer’s thought and its impact on later
6807: nineteenth-century intellectual life that terms like savagery, barbarism
6808: and civilization remain loaded, ambiguous terms, even today.
6809: 
6810: Spencer went further. He suggested that the idea of Progress was more
6811: than just a moral belief or value judgment. The new positivist Science
6812: of Spencer’s time, building on eighteenth-century Enlightenment
6813: philosophy and observation of the natural world, had given Western
6814: society the ability to objectively measure and confirm its belief in
6815: progress. Spencer therefore gave this belief in progress a scientific
6816: mandate – the visible, scientific proof of Progress lay in observation
6817: of the world.
6818: 
6819: Further, Spencer claimed to build on the evolutionary concepts of
6820: Charles Darwin – the famous phrase ‘survival of the fittest’, often
6821: popularly attributed to Darwin, was actually coined by Spencer in his
6822: 1864 Principles of Biology. Spencer interpreted Darwin to give a mandate
6823: to ideas of cultural evolution and particularly of progress. There were
6824: undoubtedly close intellectual and cultural links in the nineteenth
6825: century between the versions of evolutionary ideas developed by Spencer
6826: and Darwin. However, later generations, particularly current proponents
6827: of versions of Darwinist evolution as applied to archaeology, draw a
6828: very sharp distinction between the two versions as we shall see.
6829: 
6830: Spencer’s ideas were popular in an age of Victorian imperialism. The
6831: thought of Spencer and others contributed to a view of the world where
6832: Europeans perceived themselves as being admirably fitted to govern
6833: subject peoples. Not only did Europeans have Christian religion and the
6834: moral order on their side, but they also believed that they were
6835: confirmed in their right to rule by the objective findings of the
6836: natural sciences. Any objective observer of the world, in this view,
6837: could see irrefutable evidence that different societies could be ranked
6838: on a ladder of progress, and further that European societies were at the
6839: top of this ladder. Native peoples such as the subjects of the British
6840: Empire might in this view aspire to progress to civilized states, but
6841: their upward progress might well be very slow, so slow as to be
6842: imperceptible to the contemporary observer, just as upward progress had
6843: been slow in the origins of civilization in the first place.
6844: 
6845: Spencer’s ideas were also popular among North American and European
6846: political élites in an age of class divisions, inequality and the
6847: growing consciousness and political confidence of an urban working
6848: class. Spencer argued that legislation to foster social reforms and to
6849: ameliorate inequalities was misguided, since it interfered with the
6850: natural workings of the ‘survival of the fittest’. Augustus Lane Fox
6851: Pitt-Rivers, often seen as the father of British field archaeology,
6852: argued strongly that if evolutionary principles were properly understood
6853: by ordinary people, they would desist from pursuing deluded notions of
6854: reform and revolution. Pitt-Rivers, then, organized the display of
6855: artefacts in his museum along evolutionary lines to educate the public
6856: in these principles.
6857: 
6858: It is worth elaborating upon this point. It has been suggested that
6859: evolutionary ideas that treat human history in terms of a ‘league table’
6860: of progress have always been popular when the intellectual basis of a
6861: culture is feeling self-confident. Thus, ideas of progress were popular
6862: in Victorian Britain, but waned in the twentieth century with two world
6863: wars and the end of the British Empire; whereas in the United States,
6864: they were especially popular during the 1960s, but lost popularity after
6865: the military and political disaster of Vietnam.
6866: 
6867: Cultural Evolution
6868: 
6869: It is difficult to over-estimate the impact of cultural evolutionary
6870: ideas on the development of archaeological thought. Many different
6871: schema of social development are ‘evolutionary’ even if they do not
6872: necessarily spell this out explicitly.
6873: 
6874: Ideas of cultural evolution were formative in the development of
6875: archaeology as a discipline in the later nineteenth century. Lubbock’s
6876: great book Prehistoric Times, for example, published in 1865, was
6877: sub-titled As Illustrated by Ancient Remains, and the Manners and
6878: Customs of Modern Savages. In other words, Lubbock brought order and
6879: system to prehistory by making the assumption that societies moved from
6880: simple to complex, and therefore that ‘the manners and customs of modern
6881: savages’ could illustrate earlier periods of British and European
6882: prehistory.
6883: 
6884: But if one stands back from this nineteenth-century context and
6885: considers evolutionary principles in abstract terms, there is no a
6886: priori theoretical reason why the direction of cultural evolution should
6887: be from simple to complex. Most schemes of cultural evolution tend to
6888: classify societies in this way, on the grounds that this, empirically,
6889: is the story of human development: from ‘simple’ hunter-gatherer forms
6890: many thousands of years ago to the ‘complex’ modern states of today. For
6891: Leslie White, this move from simple to complex was explained as a
6892: tendency to harness ever more vast quantities of energy, as cultural
6893: systems became more entropic or tightly integrated. Durkheim’s and
6894: Spencer’s views of evolution also specified a definite direction from
6895: simple to complex. In many versions of evolutionary theory, however,
6896: there is no reason why social forms cannot move in the other direction
6897: in certain circumstances, that is from complex to simple, just as
6898: organic forms can do in biology. This is what is proposed with the
6899: collapse of cultural systems and their ‘return’ to ‘simpler’ forms of
6900: society, like the Ancient Maya after the ‘Classic Maya Collapse’ or the
6901: ancestral pueblos of Chaco Canyon.
6902: 
6903: There is also no a priori theoretical reason why more complex forms of
6904: society are better adapted to the natural environment than less complex
6905: forms. If modern capitalist society is wiped out in, say, excesses of
6906: industrial pollution, or in global warming, it may well be the case that
6907: ‘simpler’ forms of human society based on subsistence agriculture or on
6908: gathering and hunting will be proven to be more adaptive in the long run
6909: to this planet’s environment than ‘complex’ industrial forms.
6910: 
6911: Third, there is no a priori reason why change should not be very sudden
6912: rather than gradual. The Victorian view of cultural evolution was
6913: ideologically saturated with the notion of change as gradual as it was
6914: inevitable. This equation of evolution with gradual change continues to
6915: be very strong in popular circles (one reads of modern institutions and
6916: companies proposing periods of ‘evolutionary change’, by which they mean
6917: that change is to be gradual and incremental rather than sudden).
6918: However, there is no reason why change should not be very sudden and
6919: transformative – as it was, arguably, in cases of the formation of early
6920: states or of systems collapse.
6921: 
6922: Finally, to state the obvious, there is no reason why we should accept
6923: Spencer’s and Durkheim’s insistence that cultural evolution leads to a
6924: more moral, just or civilized society. It is perfectly possible to take
6925: the view that while in practice, human societies have moved from less
6926: complex to more complex forms, this tells us nothing about the moral
6927: worth of those societies most advocates of cultural evolution today
6928: would insist on this qualification. Indeed, Romantic thinkers often
6929: posit the reverse – a moral decline attendant upon social complexity and
6930: specifically industrial society, the loss of a more authentic and less
6931: alienated way of life that is in some way supposed to be ‘closer to
6932: Nature’.
6933: 
6934: It is important to stress that different evolutionary schema can be very
6935: different from one another, both in their classifications and in the
6936: suggested mechanisms for social transition. Some evolutionary schemes
6937: are unilinear; that is, they suggest that there is one broad pattern or
6938: trend to cultural evolution. Others are multilinear, suggesting a number
6939: of divergent evolutionary paths a particular cultural sequence can
6940: follow. These multilinear schemes can diverge or converge (figure 9.4).
6941: 
6942: Figure 9.4 Clarke’s contrast (drawn from Kroeber 1948) between organic
6943: and cultural evolution
6944: 
6945: [c09_image004.jpg]
6946: 
6947: We might contrast for example the two unilinear schemes of the cultural
6948: anthropologists Elman Service and Morton Fried. Both were compiled by
6949: assembling many examples of different human societies from ethnographic
6950: research, and both have been particularly influential on archaeologists.
6951: Service gives us a fourfold typology ranging along the scale of simple
6952: to complex of band, tribe, chiefdom and state. Fried offers an
6953: alternative scheme of egalitarian, ranked, stratified and state. Note
6954: that while they differ in their terminology, the descriptions of Service
6955: and Fried share a great deal of common ground. Both start and stop at
6956: the same point (they start with ‘simple’ gatherer-hunter societies,
6957: though their definitions of such societies differ, and end with the
6958: modern state). They both also share a similar methodology; in other
6959: words, the methods that have been used to draw them up are very much the
6960: same: Service and Fried collated many different ethnographic examples of
6961: different social systems, and then ranked or classified them according
6962: to perceived criteria of complexity.
6963: 
6964: Note that this is another example of archaeology being influenced by
6965: thinking developed in other disciplines. In practice, much earlier
6966: archaeology within the tradition of cultural evolution has followed the
6967: method of ascertaining what the archaeological correlates of each stage
6968: might be, and then looking for them in the archaeological record. For
6969: example, Service suggests that chiefdoms will include the following
6970: features among others: a settlement hierarchy; redistributive trade
6971: networks; religious complexity involving monumental construction and so
6972: on. Much archaeological effort has been spent in refining these criteria
6973: and seeking out relevant evidence in the archaeological record in a wide
6974: variety of contexts, from Renfrew’s work on Neolithic and Early Bronze
6975: Age Wessex to Tim Earle’s work on chiefdoms in Southern America and
6976: Polynesia.
6977: 
6978: In a more recent example, Raymond Kelly studied a range of different ‘p
6979: re-state’ societies. He was interested in the relationship between war
6980: on the one hand, and social segmentation on the other. By comparing
6981: different segmented and unsegmented societies, Kelly concluded that
6982: segmentation of societies was related to the development of a group
6983: mentality, which in turn enabled and facilitated inter-village raiding.
6984: Kelly’s general scheme was then applied to archaeological data from
6985: ancient Mexico by Flannery and Marcus (2003).
6986: 
6987: One of the great strengths of cultural evolutionary theory, from the
6988: point of view of North American archaeology, is that it strongly
6989: integrates archaeology within the discipline of anthropology as a whole.
6990: It suggests and facilitates cultural anthropologists and
6991: ‘anthropological archaeologists’ working together in a strong
6992: collaborative programme into what are perceived as important, concrete,
6993: empirical questions. These questions are generalizing and comparative in
6994: nature and are of relevance to the modern world (ranking, warfare, the
6995: origins of the State). Within this project, archaeologists are an
6996: important and central part of the wider thrust of a generalizing
6997: anthropological theory.
6998: 
6999: Criticisms of Cultural Evolution
7000: 
7001: Many of the criticisms of cultural evolution echo postprocessual
7002: critiques of archaeology as a whole. Shanks and Tilley, for example,
7003: write: ‘The concepts of function, adaptation, and evolution have no
7004: explanatory role in a consideration of the social and need to be either
7005: completely abandoned or reduced to a simple descriptive vocabulary’
7006: (1987: 210). Mark Pluciennik’s 2005 Social Evolution comes very close to
7007: a complete rejection of any form of evolutionary thinking. Why do they
7008: suggest this?
7009: 
7010: First, it is argued that evolutionary schema apply criteria from
7011: ‘outside’. Polynesia, Wessex, Iron Age Denmark, the ‘Anasazi’ are all
7012: treated as belonging to the same stage of evolution, and the same
7013: factors and mechanisms for change are examined in each context – class
7014: conflict, environmental stress, development of a managerial élite, and
7015: so on. Cultural evolutionary models, it is therefore argued, ‘flatten
7016: out’ past societies. Past people’s own views of themselves are, it is
7017: argued, seen as unimportant, and the unique qualities of particular
7018: cultures – for example, their artistic or cultural achievements – are
7019: not seen as important or capable of producing generalizations. In short,
7020: critics of cultural evolution suggest that such schema find it difficult
7021: to encompass the particularity of historical sequences.
7022: 
7023: There is a moral and political dimension to this point. Indigenous
7024: peoples, the ‘subjects’ of comparative ethnography, often feel that the
7025: unique and particular qualities of their ancestral traditions and modes
7026: of life are ignored or suppressed in this flattening-out. Further, their
7027: mode of life is presented not as vibrant and meaningful, worthy of study
7028: in its own right. Rather, it is treated as some relic of a past and
7029: outmoded way of living; the only way forward for them, in such a scheme,
7030: is to move forward in the evolutionary scheme by embracing ‘modern’
7031: Western ways of living.
7032: 
7033: Second, earlier cultural evolutionary models often seem to leave little
7034: room for contingency or historical accident. Societies seem to be on a
7035: smooth, high, inexorable road to state formation. Critics argue that
7036: history could have happened otherwise, purely by chance. A favourite
7037: variant of this claim is that all such models are implicitly
7038: teleological. A teleological view of history is one in which there is a
7039: strong sense of direction, of humanity moving inexorably towards a
7040: predefined and predetermined goal (telos).
7041: 
7042: Third, in their simpler forms, cultural evolutionary models tend to
7043: ignore diffusion and cultural contact. If each society goes through a
7044: fundamentally similar set of stages, what account is given of contact
7045: between societies? How might such contact alter or deflect social
7046: change?
7047: 
7048: Fourth, it is argued that the individual is also ignored. Human beings
7049: are presented as pawns, locked into an inexorable evolutionary process
7050: over which they have no control. Again, in parallel with systems
7051: thinking, evolutionary models are open to the attack of critical
7052: theorists like Habermas and others discussed in chapter 5.
7053: 
7054: As a result of these criticisms and responses to them, simple models of
7055: cultural evolution, particularly uninilear ones, have been all but
7056: abandoned. Archaeologists using evolutionary theory have taken one of
7057: two courses: they have either made the attempt to abandon evolutionary
7058: theory entirely (such as many postprocessualists), or alternatively have
7059: attempted to develop cultural evolutionary ideas to take account of
7060: these criticisms. As I discuss this latter attempt below, the student
7061: will observe that some of these developed forms, particularly different
7062: strands of multilinear evolution, link up with the modifications to
7063: systems thinking discussed in chapter 5.
7064: 
7065: Multilinear Evolution
7066: 
7067: All the evolutionary schemes discussed above suggest that there is one
7068: basic scale, simple to complex, and societies move up and down that
7069: scale. However, as indicated in figure 9.4, one can propose that there
7070: can be more than one scale or pathway. Trajectories of social change can
7071: miss out particular stages, particularly as the result of contact or
7072: diffusion between societies. Or there might be a much looser typology of
7073: different social practices.
7074: 
7075: These points were developed in the earlier part of the twentieth
7076: century, particularly in a backlash in North America against the schema
7077: of classical sociocultural evolution discussed above. In particular, the
7078: anthropologist Franz Boas led criticism of unilinear evolution as
7079: ethnocentric, dependent on flawed ethnography, and harnessed to an
7080: unacceptable idea of Progress. As a result of Boas’ and others’
7081: criticisms, for much of the twentieth century, cultural evolution was at
7082: best a minority pursuit among North American anthropologists, though we
7083: have seen how this minority, particularly Leslie White, had a profound
7084: effect on New Archaeology.
7085: 
7086: As a result, later forms of cultural evolution were often ‘multilinear’
7087: rather than ‘unilinear’ in character. What does this mean? First, and
7088: most obviously, multinear evolution proposes that societies can take
7089: different paths or trajectories. These trajectories may or may not lead
7090: to a common end-point, for example State societies. Or they may lead in
7091: quite different directions.
7092: 
7093: More subtly, proponents of multilinear approaches pursue alternative
7094: ways of thinking to the grand evolutionary stages of classic
7095: sociocultural evolution. For example, early discussion of ‘chiefdoms’
7096: took chiefdom society as an intermediate stage in unlinear evolution,
7097: between earlier and state societies. However, chiefdoms might be seen
7098: equally well in the interstices of social formations – the networks of
7099: contemporary drug barons, pirates, warlords and gang leaders, for
7100: example, fit many of the criteria of ‘chiefdom societies’, but clearly
7101: exist within the wider social networks of modern states.
7102: 
7103: It is also possible to attempt to decouple the question of social
7104: complexity from the more specific features of hierarchy and
7105: stratification. Carole Crumley has argued that social complexity need
7106: not imply a rigid hierarchy or order. Rather, complex societies are
7107: characterized by ‘heterarchy’: ‘structures are heterarchical when each
7108: element is either unranked relative to other elements or possesses the
7109: potential for being ranked in a number of different ways’ (Crumley 1987,
7110: 158). In this view, hierarchy is only one possible form of heterarchy,
7111: and in practice, most societies exhibit a range of different ranked and
7112: unranked forms.
7113: 
7114: As a result of the criticisms raised above, and as a result of
7115: development in evolutionary thought as a whole, most of those working in
7116: cultural evolution have abandoned any simple idea of stages. Much work
7117: within evolutionary traditions has looked at diverse trends or
7118: trajectories of development. It has also looked at evolution not as a
7119: monolithic process or as a simple ladder of classification. It has
7120: attempted to include accounts of agency, practice theory, factional
7121: competition, and conflict in its models, and located the source of
7122: generalizing inferences not in formal comparisons or rankings of
7123: societies on a notional scale, but rather in thinking carefully about
7124: process. It is interested in corporate entities and the role of
7125: individual identity and factional politics. It has attempted to link up
7126: state formation on the one hand and rhythms of daily life on the other.
7127: 
7128: Cultural Evolution and Marxism
7129: 
7130: As we have seen with the constellation of Darwin, Marx and Spencer,
7131: there is a great deal of common ground between much of evolutionary
7132: thought and classical Marxism. Marxism can be seen as one form of
7133: cultural evolutionary theory, and conversely, much cultural evolutionary
7134: theory can be seen as a form of Marxist thought, though often the
7135: Marxist content is implicit or coded. Indeed, Leslie White was attacked
7136: in the McCarthyite paranoia of his time for his political beliefs,
7137: though conversely he was accused of watering down his Marxism by others
7138: such as Maurice Bloch. Several of the autobiographical accounts of New
7139: Archaeologists make it clear that cultural evolutionary ideas of the
7140: 1960s were discussed and advocated within a North American intellectual
7141: and cultural environment in which Marxism played a very significant
7142: role, even if this was not overtly acknowledged at the time.
7143: 
7144: Classical Marxism, as discussed in earlier chapters, proposes that all
7145: societies go through the same set of stages: primitive communist,
7146: ancient, feudal, capitalist. Marx and Engels, indeed, read and were
7147: profoundly influenced by the thought of several nineteenth-century
7148: evolutionary thinkers, most notably Maine and Morgan. Marx wanted to
7149: dedicate Das Kapital to Charles Darwin, though Darwin declined the
7150: honour.
7151: 
7152: Cultural evolution also shares many of the problems of classical
7153: Marxism. One of these is that in its ‘classical’, unilinear form,
7154: Marxism allows little or no room for contact between societies. If
7155: societies evolve from one state to another, what role is there for
7156: cultural contact and diffusion? Others have argued that to propose one
7157: single scheme of evolution is simplistic. Societies might develop along
7158: different paths.
7159: 
7160: One particular form of Marxism which engages with evolutionary questions
7161: is ‘Latin American social archaeology’. Latin American social
7162: archaeology had its origins in the 1970s and 1980s as an application of
7163: Marxist, and implicitly evolutionary, ideas to the archaeological
7164: record, particularly via Gordon Childe’s discussion of social evolution.
7165: Latin American social archaeologists make an analytical division between
7166: socio-economic formation, mode of life or modo de vida, and culture.
7167: 
7168: The first of these three categories, socio-economic formation, refers to
7169: the general historical stage of a society; the last, ‘culture’, refers
7170: to its particular properties. The intermediate category, modo de vida,
7171: refers to the range of different modes of existence that can occur
7172: within a general socio-economic formation. For example, within the
7173: general socio-economic formation of hunter-gatherer societies, one might
7174: have different forms of modo de vida according to different relative
7175: stresses on hunting and gathering, and still more different particular
7176: examples of different ‘cultures’, each with particular features. Latin
7177: American social archaeology, by dividing social analysis in this way,
7178: makes the claim that it can combine forms of unilinear and multilinear
7179: evolution within a single framework of analysis.
7180: 
7181: A second form of thinking relevant in this context is political economy.
7182: Political economy, as a general term, refers to the relations between
7183: political and economic practices and systems. It was developed as a body
7184: of thought by Karl Marx, who drew on earlier ‘classical economists’ such
7185: as David Ricardo and Adam Smith. Key themes developed by
7186: nineteenth-century political economists included: the importance and
7187: nature of property rights, for example over land; the way value is
7188: created within different economic systems; the division of labour, and
7189: its relationship to issues of alienation. Marx’s particular interest was
7190: in modern capitalist societies, but political economy has been developed
7191: within archaeology with reference to pre-State and early State
7192: societies.
7193: 
7194: Archaeologists taking an approach derived from political economy are
7195: interested in different economic practices (the provision of labour
7196: services, subsistence agriculture, household economy, gift exchange,
7197: circulation of prestige goods). They explore how these practices
7198: intersect with issues and processes of political power (ranking, the
7199: rise and collapse of chiefdoms, state formation). Archaeological studies
7200: of political economy take a broadly evolutionary approach, in that they
7201: engage with, for example, the economic processes that structure social
7202: ranking, and especially the processes and structures that are involved
7203: in state formation (see below). They are also part of the broader body
7204: of approaches that developed out of earlier systems thinking discussed
7205: in chapter 5.
7206: 
7207: Origins of the State
7208: 
7209: An important, even central, strand of thinking in cultural evolution has
7210: been the development of generalizing and comparative theories of the
7211: origin of the State. It can be argued that ‘state origins’, or more
7212: broadly the origins of social complexity, is one of the Big Questions in
7213: archaeology, alongside the origins of homo sapiens sapiens and the
7214: origins of agriculture. States are generally defined in terms of their
7215: use of force and their legitimation of political inequality. In the
7216: archaeological record, it is argued by evolutionary theorists, states
7217: can be seen as having bureacracies, settlement hierarchies, palaces and
7218: temples, and as seeking to build up territory and Empire. Many
7219: archaeologists working on state origins insist that most or all of these
7220: features can be seen cross-culturally, in contexts of ‘primary state
7221: formation’ ranging from Mesoamerica to Mesopotamia to China.
7222: 
7223: For example, a relatively traditional study is Spencer and Redmond’s
7224: review of the evidence for state formation in Mesoamerica. Spencer takes
7225: as given the unilinear scale of progress between chiefdom and state, and
7226: he is working within an established tradition that maintains that
7227: distinct archaeological correlates can be seen for each. They review
7228: archaeological work in different areas, organizing it ‘according to
7229: three diagnostic criteria: a) the emergence of a four-tier regional
7230: settlement-size hierarchy; b) the appearance of royal palaces and
7231: specialised temples; and c) the conquest/subjugation of distant
7232: territories’. They conclude, for example, that:
7233: 
7234: for the La Venta region, surveyors have reported a three-tier settlement
7235: hierarchy for the Middle Preclassic period. No convincing plans of
7236: palaces or standardized, multiroom temples have resulted from
7237: excavations … There is no compelling evidence that the Olmecs of the
7238: Southern Gulf Coast carried out an aggressive takeover of any distant
7239: region. We conclude that the Formative period Olmecs are appropriately
7240: interpreted as chiefdoms, not states. (Spencer and Redmond 2004: 175,
7241: 187).
7242: 
7243: In a classic example of how cultural evolution has attempted to move
7244: beyond unilinear models, Baines and Yoffee have stressed how early state
7245: societies were not all the same as one another. They shift emphasis away
7246: from comparing specific societies in terms of ticking off a check-list
7247: of different features such as bureaucracies and temples, and towards
7248: developing generalizing comments about processes ‘through an
7249: investigation of the instrumental principles of hierarchisation, of the
7250: restriction and display of certain kinds of wealth and the devaluation
7251: of other kinds of symbols, and of constitutive institutions of
7252: legitimation that emphasise the dispersive ties between rulers and
7253: ruled’ (Baines and Yoffee 2000: 13).
7254: 
7255: Again, Adam T. Smith has discussed the relationship between the
7256: constitution of landscape and political authority in early states. He
7257: rightly criticizes earlier evolutionary models for treating space as
7258: unimportant – that is, comparing Mesopotamia with Mesoamerica with
7259: China, without thinking with sufficient care about the landscape
7260: differences between these very different locales. However, Smith does
7261: not forsake a generalizing approach, and ends up with a general stress
7262: on the importance of landscape and the centrality of political authority
7263: rather than ‘environment’ as generalizing comments on what he calls
7264: ‘early complex polities’ – though it is striking that Smith ends up
7265: being equivocal about the utility of this general term (Smith 2003:
7266: 278).
7267: 
7268: Case Study: Cahokia
7269: 
7270: Cahokia is a complex of around 120 earthen mounds and other features in
7271: the midwestern United States, near Collinsville, Illinois in the
7272: American Bottom floodplain, across the Mississippi River from St Louis,
7273: Missouri; the site was occupied between ad 650 and 1400. Cahokia
7274: dominates the surrounding landscape, being the largest site of its kind
7275: by far; at its peak, the site was the largest settlement north of
7276: Mexico. The central monument, Monk’s Mound, is the largest humanly
7277: constructed earthen mound in the Americas, big enough to be termed by
7278: some a pyramid (figure 9.5); it is one of around 120 mounds. The whole
7279: site may have had a population of up to 20,000 people.
7280: 
7281: The interpretation of Cahokia, and the ‘Mississippian culture’ of the
7282: surrounding region, has become a classic example of the development and
7283: application of theoretical ideas. Early studies concentrated on
7284: description, chronology and ‘culture history’. The development of
7285: cultural evolutionary models led archaeologists to debate whether
7286: Cahokia could be classified as a chiefdom or as an early state, and if
7287: so, how complex it was, whether it could be described as a ‘city’. Thus
7288: studies of the site tended to maximize or minimize its size and
7289: complexity, while studies of the surrounding region either stressed how
7290: dominant and far-reaching Cahokia’s power and influence was, or
7291: conversely, tended to minimize this dominance and argue that the site
7292: was the centre of no more than a ‘complex chiefdom’ (Milner 1998: 3).
7293: 
7294: Figure 9.5 Artist’s impression of the site of Cahokia at its peak, by
7295: William R. Iseminger
7296: 
7297: [c09_image005.jpg]
7298: 
7299: Cobb comments on the study of the Mississippian culture in terms that
7300: could be extended to the study of other areas and periods:
7301: 
7302: If there is a historical trend in studies of Mississippian complexity it
7303: is one that first emphasized hierarchy. This was followed by a
7304: consideration of the horizontal power links, the heterarchical
7305: structure, between segments of society. Finally, some researchers have
7306: moved to a practice-based approach that emphasizes agency and relations
7307: more than system or structure. Yet these changes in perspective
7308: represent more of a Doppler shift than a complete reorientation –
7309: scholars may represent different areas on the spectrum, but they do not
7310: seem to have lost sight of the importance of the spectrum itself (Cobb
7311: 2003: 79).
7312: 
7313: The most recent assessment of Cahokia exemplifies this trend: Julie
7314: Zimmerman Holt’s characterization of a ‘theatre state’ (2009: 231). Holt
7315: is struck by the similarity of the monuments and associated practices at
7316: Cahokia to the description by the cultural anthropologist Clifford
7317: Geertz of the Balinese ‘theatre state’. Holt hinges her argument on
7318: specific crosscultural comparisons in her use of Geertz’s Balinese
7319: material, and she draws attention to both archaeological and historic
7320: evidence for sacrificial and other rituals, including human sacrifice,
7321: and games such as chunkey. In so doing, Holt brings together many of the
7322: recent developments in thinking about cultural evolution and state
7323: origins. She asserts: a) a questioning of simplistic models and accepted
7324: categories of cultural evolution, for example a cut-and-dried
7325: distinction between ‘chiefdom’ and ‘state’; b) a rejection of the
7326: distinction between power and ritual as a false choice; c) a rethinking
7327: of stress on the importance of theatre from Geertzian anthropology; and
7328: d) an implied stress on the importance of practice – including
7329: strategies used by leaders and commoners to such as ‘group-building’ or
7330: ‘group-distincting’. In this way, an interpretive stress on agency and
7331: practice has been built into a wider, generalizing and comparative
7332: framework.
7333: 
7334: Cultural Evolution Strikes Back
7335: 
7336: My response to the debate over the utility of cultural evolutionary
7337: models is to observe that, however much they have been overtly abandoned
7338: by some scholars, archaeologists resort to underlying ideas of cultural
7339: evolution far more frequently than they sometimes care to admit. Those
7340: underlying ideas are often unacknowledged, and are often of the most
7341: simple and basic kind, tending back, implicitly, to unilinear models.
7342: 
7343: The key tension in current thinking, then, is not between archaeologists
7344: who affirm cultural evolutionary principles and those who disavow them,
7345: but rather between different elements of what all archaeologists do and
7346: what they claim that they do – a tension that is part of a broader
7347: tension in contemporary archaeology that I will return to in the
7348: concluding chapter.
7349: 
7350: Most obviously, the terminology used to describe and classify different
7351: cultural stages and phases remains that coined by an earlier,
7352: evolutionary age. Terms like archaic, formative, classic, and
7353: post-classic still abound. An excellent reason for the survival of these
7354: terms is simple inertia, or a dependence on a common terminology or set
7355: of reference points. For an archaeologist who has grown up with
7356: classifications of the material framed in this way, it is arguably a
7357: waste of intellectual time and effort to attempt to get academic
7358: consensus for a renaming. Whether archaeologists like it or not, the
7359: classification and terminology of Stone, Bronze and Iron Ages will still
7360: be with us in the decades and centuries to come; Classical civilization
7361: will still be Classical civilization and the Dark Ages will still be the
7362: Dark Ages; the Palaeolithic will still be divided into Lower, Middle and
7363: Upper.
7364: 
7365: Some archaeologists would claim that these terms are no more than simple
7366: descriptive usages. However, one very compelling point of the critiques
7367: outlined in chapters 7 and 8 is that language is more important than
7368: this. It is naïve to see terms as ‘unimportant’ or ‘just descriptive’,
7369: just as it is naïve to attempt to dismiss the sexist use of language as
7370: unimportant. However if it is accepted that the terminology persists, it
7371: follows that the older schemes of unlinear evolution are not simply
7372: passé; they continue to structure archaeological thought in often
7373: unacknowledged ways.
7374: 
7375: However, I suggest that what is at stake here is much more than the
7376: problems of terminology. Let us take as an example phenomenological
7377: interpretations of Neolithic and Bronze Age Wessex, of the sort
7378: discussed at the end of chapter 7. Such accounts are often laid out with
7379: implicit reference to a strong position of overt rejection of
7380: evolutionary theory and generalizing models of archaeological process.
7381: However, interpretations of people moving across the landscape, what
7382: they see and experience at different points, and what meanings and
7383: understandings those experiences related to, have been set out against a
7384: backdrop of an implicit, shared understanding of what Neolithic and
7385: Bronze Age society and culture was like – what sort of social forms
7386: operated, how society worked at that time.
7387: 
7388: For example, visual references to tombs of different kinds are
7389: interpreted as references to the ‘ancestors’. These arguments are only
7390: convincing if one thinks it plausible or compelling to propose that
7391: links to the ancestors conferred territorial rights, or were pertinent
7392: to territorial claims; or more loosely that kin ties and rights over
7393: land were intimately related. Such a general understanding of Wessex
7394: society is clearly derived from, at the very least, a general awareness
7395: of comparative ethnography, of groups around the world, even if that
7396: general awareness is not overtly referenced. But for such examples to be
7397: relevant, and for the reasoning to convince an archaeological audience,
7398: it must be assumed that there is some general comparability between this
7399: prehistoric past and the ethnographic present. It is difficult to see
7400: how this implicit assumption is justified, unless some model of cultural
7401: evolution, however vague and unstated, is being deployed.
7402: 
7403: It can be argued, moreover, that some kind of engagement with the issues
7404: raised by cultural evolution, however critical and qualified that
7405: engagement is, necessarily starts the moment one asks ‘why?’ questions.
7406: Take, for example, my account of the medieval open hall, which was
7407: presented in chapter 7 as an example of interpretive archaeology. The
7408: obvious next question, which is frequently asked of me by colleagues
7409: raised in evolutionary traditions, is: why was the open hall abandoned
7410: when it was, in the sixteenth century? To answer this question properly,
7411: one has to refer outwards – to, for example, a change in underlying
7412: sentiment and mentality, for example a ‘rise in privacy’. But why a rise
7413: in privacy? Well, a greater sense of the individual, related in turn to
7414: Renaissance ideas and the rise of market relations. Why the rise of the
7415: market? And so one goes on: ultimately it is very difficult to avoid
7416: being drawn into some kind of generalizing account of
7417: feudalism-to-capitalism that is at the very least influenced by Marxist
7418: and other evolutionary accounts of general social change.
7419: 
7420: At a more basic and general level, it is possible to identify the
7421: assumptions of cultural evolution at an implicit level within much of
7422: archaeological discourse, particularly when that discourse claims not to
7423: be theoretical. Many such discussions of the ‘rise of civilization’, for
7424: example, in treating it as a narrative rather than questioning its
7425: causes, see the rise of civilization as a natural and inevitable
7426: process: traditional accounts of the rise of the Roman Empire are a case
7427: in point. Again, many ‘common-sense’ discussions of technology make the
7428: assumption that technology simply gets better and better through
7429: ‘natural qualities’ of human ingenuity and problem-solving. Ironically,
7430: then, ideas of cultural evolution are most tenacious, and evolutionary
7431: ideas are expressed in their most simplistic and questionable form, in
7432: areas of the discipline that consider themselves to be free from
7433: ‘theory’.
7434: 
7435: In my view, then, claims of the death of cultural evolutionary theory
7436: have been over-stated. It is very difficult to specify an interpretation
7437: of the past which is broadly social and processual which does not, at
7438: some level, rest its foundations on assumptions about cultural
7439: evolution. It is arguable that there has been a relative decline in the
7440: quantity of archaeological literature that deals explicitly with
7441: questions of cultural evolution, whether of unilinear or multilinear
7442: strands. There is certainly a major difference in the profile of
7443: evolutionary ideas between North America, where cultural evolution
7444: remains an important and even dominant tradition, and much of the rest
7445: of the world, especially Britain and the rest of Europe. However, I
7446: would argue that cultural evolutionary ideas continue to underpin a very
7447: large part of what archaeologists actually do, whether we like it or
7448: not. I will come back to how this tension might be negotiated in the
7449: concluding chapter.
7450: 
7451: 10
7452: 
7453: Archaeology and Darwinian Evolution
7454: 
7455: If you think you understand [evolution], you don’t know nearly enough
7456: about it … evolution must be the best-known yet worst-understood of all
7457: scientific theories (LePage 2008: 24).
7458: 
7459: I traced in the last chapter how evolutionary ideas were a central
7460: element of the intellectual origins and basis of archaeology as a
7461: discipline. Further, I tried to show those ideas were given a decisive
7462: cast during the decades of the mid to late nineteenth century, an
7463: intellectual framework that created the intellectual foundations of
7464: archaeology as a discipline. The chapter then diverged, choosing to
7465: follow Spencer’s cultural evolution rather than self-styled ‘Darwinian
7466: strands’ of evolutionary archaeology.
7467: 
7468: In this chapter, I will start by describing a few elements of Darwin’s
7469: thought. Without Darwin, archaeology would not be the discipline it is
7470: today. We noted in the last chapter how ideas of cultural evolution
7471: continue to structure much archaeological enquiry whether overtly or
7472: implicitly. Much the same can be said of Darwinian ideas. These are
7473: often described as controversial in archaeology and in their application
7474: to human culture generally, and indeed many of them are. However, it is
7475: equally true to say that the intellectual framework encompassed by the
7476: thought of Darwin remains absolutely foundational to much of the
7477: discipline of archaeology, and foundational to intellectual life in
7478: general. With apologies to Harold Macmillan, we are all Darwinians now;
7479: Darwin’s thought established a cultural horizon without which
7480: archaeology, and the human sciences, would not exist in the form they
7481: take today.
7482: 
7483: The long-term impact of Darwinian ideas has been sharpened in the last
7484: 20 years. In this period, a number of explicitly ‘Darwinian’
7485: archaeologies have flourished, in tandem with the heightened profile of
7486: Darwinist evolutionary approaches to the human sciences as a whole.
7487: Theoretical writing styling itself Darwinian has ranged from general
7488: outlooks on the human sciences as an intellectual project, to
7489: specifically archaeological theories and approaches, to particular
7490: methods and research strategies, to particular explanations of elements
7491: of the archaeological record or episodes in the past.
7492: 
7493: In their many forms, different Darwinian ‘archaeologies’ often look very
7494: different from one another. Their starting assumptions and methods often
7495: seem to be very varied to the outside observer, and their style, tone
7496: and rhetoric often differ very markedly. It is therefore impossible to
7497: summarize a single Darwinian or evolutionary archaeology, perhaps even
7498: more so than the difficulties in generalizing about other schools such
7499: as cultural evolutionary thought or postprocessual archaeology. However,
7500: all these theories share one thing in common: a claim to be descended
7501: from the thought of Charles Darwin and his theory of evolution.
7502: 
7503: Darwin’s Dangerous Idea
7504: 
7505: Darwin published The Origin of Species in 1859. It is difficult to
7506: over-state the excitement and impact of this book, in both scientific
7507: and cultural terms. The Origin of Species was a central element in the
7508: constellation of ideas discussed in the last chapter. It was the
7509: outspoken manifesto of a view of the world that was explicitly and
7510: unashamedly scientific and evolutionary. Its arguments, and those of
7511: Darwin’s later The Descent of Man, resonated not just through the
7512: sciences, but across society as a whole, provoking responses from the
7513: shocked to the vituperative to the satirical (figure 10.1). Spencer,
7514: Marx and others applied evolutionary ideas to the study of human
7515: society.
7516: 
7517: Darwin’s ideas filtered through into the arts and humanities also. The
7518: poet Tennyson wrote of ‘Nature red in tooth and claw’ ten years before
7519: Origin was published, but Tennyson was influenced by proto-Darwinist
7520: ideas, and the phrase spread rapidly, like a virus or what some
7521: Darwinists would call a meme. Many of the artist and critic John
7522: Ruskin’s ideas about aesthetics were framed in bitter opposition to
7523: Darwin, while Spencer’s phrase ‘survival of the fittest’ resonated
7524: through thinking on philosophy and human culture. It is arguable who
7525: influenced whom within this maelstrom of ideas; what is clear is that
7526: all these ideas formed an intellectual horizon in nineteenth-century
7527: thinking whose importance cannot be overemphasized. In all this rapid
7528: take-up of evolutionary ideas across a range of disciplines, it is easy
7529: to lose sight of the core elements of evolution as laid out by Darwin
7530: himself.
7531: 
7532: Evolution has been defined as ‘descent with modification’ (Mace, Holden
7533: and Shennan 2005: 2). Darwin proposed that plants and animals pass on
7534: their characteristics to their offspring. Different offspring, however,
7535: vary from one another. (Darwin was unsure of why this happened, and it
7536: is interesting to note this gap in nineteenth-century evolutionary
7537: thinking; it fell to later generations of scientists to discover and
7538: explore the cause of biological variation, namely the operation of genes
7539: and genetic mutation.)
7540: 
7541: Figure 10.1 Charles Darwin, from a cartoon in 1871
7542: 
7543: [c10_image001.jpg]
7544: 
7545: The resulting variations between different plants and animals mean that,
7546: by chance, some are better suited to their environment than others. The
7547: ones that are better suited to their environment stand a better chance
7548: of growing and surviving, and a better chance, critically, of
7549: reproducing, and thus passing on that variation to succeeding
7550: generations. Those plants and animals that are more successful in
7551: reproducing will, in time, expand in numbers, and come to drive out
7552: their ‘competitors’.
7553: 
7554: Darwin’s theory was quite simple in its essentials, but exceptionally
7555: powerful, and exceptionally dangerous in terms of its implications for
7556: understanding the natural and human world. It provided a basis for the
7557: scientific explanation of observed variation in the natural world; no
7558: serious scholar now disputes its basic principles. However, I want to
7559: focus here on the cultural impact of Darwin’s ideas, since (as
7560: evolutionary thinkers frequently complain), while they remain as an
7561: accepted basis for biological evolution, their implications are often
7562: under-appreciated in the human sciences.
7563: 
7564: First, and perhaps most fundamentally, Darwin’s theory demystified the
7565: natural world. For nineteenth-century readers of evolution, the natural
7566: world was no longer simply or only God’s work, a mysterious source of
7567: eternal wonder to be understood through divine revelation. Rather, the
7568: natural world was to be understood with reference to Science. In my
7569: view, all the current debates over the implications of Darwin’s findings
7570: for belief in God miss the more important point. Whatever one’s
7571: religious beliefs might be, as a scientist or scholar, the world was now
7572: much more open and amenable to rational and empirical enquiry than it
7573: was before Darwin’s theory. Different readers of this book will disagree
7574: about whether or not the world is ultimately to be understood as God’s
7575: creation, but it is a matter of scientific agreement that the natural
7576: world has within it abundant evidence for a series of processes that
7577: explain its tremendous variation in evolutionary terms.
7578: 
7579: When viewed with hindsight, Darwinian thought can be held to represent
7580: the logical end-point of a process of opening up the world to scientific
7581: enquiry that can trace its origins back to early modern science and
7582: natural philosophy. In the sixteenth century, Copernicus demonstrated
7583: that the earth moved round the sun and not the other way around. By
7584: doing so, he destroyed the traditional conception of an earth-centred
7585: universe. More broadly, the great empiricist philosophers such as Bacon,
7586: Hobbes and Locke insisted that the world was to be understood not
7587: through abstract thought, but through empirical observation and rational
7588: method. In so doing, their work tended, in terms of the unfolding of
7589: intellectual history, to decouple scientific enquiry and natural
7590: philosophy on the one hand from religion and theology on the other. A
7591: Darwinian view of the world, then, is fundamentally opposed to for
7592: example a Romantic view, in which emotional affinity and revelation of
7593: the nature or essence of things through mystical experience are often
7594: placed at centre stage.
7595: 
7596: Second, Darwin’s theory was able to explain tremendous variety and
7597: complexity in the natural world around him with reference to the
7598: operation and interaction of a few simple processes. The end result –
7599: the biological variety we see around us, the thousands of species of
7600: plants and animals and their interactions – was infinitely complex, and
7601: required careful and complex scientific study to elucidate. But
7602: crucially it could be explained, in its essentials, in a relatively
7603: simple way. He expressed this point in a famous passage, a tableau drawn
7604: not from his more famous voyage around the world on the Beagle, but
7605: rather from his daily walks across the green fields and hills of the
7606: English landscape around his home at Down House in Kent, south-eastern
7607: England (figure 10.2):
7608: 
7609: It is interesting to contemplate an entangled bank, clothed with many
7610: plants of various kinds, with birds singing on the bushes, with various
7611: insects flitting about, and with worms crawling through the damp earth,
7612: and to reflect that these elaborately constructed forms, so different
7613: from each other, and dependent on each other in so complex a manner,
7614: have all been produced by laws acting around us (Darwin 1859 [Wilson ed.
7615: 2006, 760]).
7616: 
7617: Figure 10.2 An entangled bank, close to Down House, Darwin’s home
7618: 
7619: [c10_image002.jpg]
7620: 
7621: Darwin’s thought, then, was reductionist. Reductionism is a term often
7622: used in a pejorative way, but I do not mean it in a pejorative sense
7623: here. Darwin made the great intellectual achievement of reducing the
7624: study of tremendous complexity and variety to the interaction of a few
7625: key laws and variables. In this sense, ideas of cultural process as
7626: discussed in chapter 5, in their stress on cutting through their ‘noise’
7627: of surface variability to the underlying cultural processes, owe a great
7628: intellectual debt to Darwin.
7629: 
7630: Reductionism of this kind is also evident in the development of
7631: nineteenth-century geology. The early date of the Industrial Revolution
7632: in Britain, and its consequent demand for raw materials such as coal,
7633: meant that the British Isles was the first part of the globe to have the
7634: details of its geology mapped out through surface observation and
7635: through examination of the sections through the earth’s crust revealed
7636: in mine shafts. The result was a beautiful, but apparently complex and
7637: confusing, mass of different rocks to be found on the surface (figure
7638: 10.3). However, this complex surface pattern was the result of an
7639: underlying stratification that is in essence very simple. The strata of
7640: the British Isles are tilted up toward the north and west, so that
7641: younger and softer rocks are exposed in the south and east. The
7642: ‘elaborately constructed forms’ of the geology and physical geography of
7643: the British Isles, then, and the world beyond, had been produced by
7644: processes which at their heart were very simple however complex the
7645: final pattern that they produced.
7646: 
7647: Figure 10.3 William ‘Strata’ Smith’s map of the geology of the British
7648: Isles
7649: 
7650: [c10_image003.jpg]
7651: 
7652: Reductionism, then, is often criticized as an intellectual process,
7653: particularly by humanists who accuse some Darwinists of simplistically
7654: reducing social and cultural variability to a biological ‘core’.
7655: However, it can be argued that properly executed, the development of
7656: reductive explanations is a necessary and unavoidable part of modern
7657: science.
7658: 
7659: Third, Darwin’s thought had radical implications for the study of
7660: humanity. It implied that homo sapiens sapiens was an animal like any
7661: other. Humans were no more ‘unique’, in principle, than any other
7662: species. The methods of study that were appropriate for the natural
7663: world, then, were logically applicable to the human world also. One of
7664: the reasons for Darwin’s impact was that he wrote in a clear and
7665: accessible style, and as a result, no reasonably intelligent reader
7666: could escape or evade the appalling implications of The Origin of
7667: Species for human descent. Indeed, in his later book The Descent of Man,
7668: Darwin went out of his way to rhetorically emphasize the point. In a
7669: passage which shocked a European world accustomed to thinking of Man as
7670: made in God’s image, he described human ancestors as fanged, hairy, even
7671: Satanic with their pointed ears and tails:
7672: 
7673: The early progenitors of man were no doubt once covered with hair, both
7674: sexes having beards: their ears were pointed and capable of movement;
7675: and their bodies were provided with a tail … our progenitors, no doubt,
7676: were arboreal in their habits, frequenting some warm, forest-clad land.
7677: The males were provided with great canine teeth, which served them as
7678: formidable weapons (Darwin 1871 [Wilson ed. 2006: 894])
7679: 
7680: Writing like this challenged claims made by scholars in other
7681: disciplines. For example assertions in the humanities that humans were
7682: in some way ‘special’ or ‘different’ from ‘mere animals’ were given a
7683: severe jolt. In this way, Darwinism can often be described as a highly
7684: positivist philosophy, in the sense of the positivist belief that the
7685: human sciences could or should follow the principles of the natural
7686: sciences.
7687: 
7688: Darwinism leads one, then, to a highly sceptical view of the idea of
7689: human essence. Claims can often be found in philosophical, theological
7690: or humanist literature about what makes us ‘distinctively human’.
7691: Darwinists are often dismissive of these claims. For Darwinists, the key
7692: question is not a meditative, philosophical or abstract one (‘what makes
7693: us human, deep down?’) but a scientific question: what combination of
7694: traits, for example large brain size, a larynx enabling complex sounds
7695: and therefore language, opposable thumbs, or primate social
7696: organization, made early hominids adaptively successful? Darwinist
7697: thought is in this sense profoundly and aggressively anti-essentialist.
7698: In earlier chapters we saw how much culture-historical or traditional
7699: archaeology is implicated in notions of what makes us ‘essentially
7700: human’, notions that again can trace an intellectual ancestry to
7701: philosophies of Romanticism and/or idealism. Darwinism encourages the
7702: view that such notions lack intellectual credibility or at best are
7703: unscientific.
7704: 
7705: Humans are, arguably, an extremely successful species, but the reasons
7706: for that success are not to be found by reference to divine will, or to
7707: particular civilized or ‘cultured’ attributes, or to some kind of human
7708: ‘essence’. Rather ‘culture’ itself can be redefined radically. For
7709: succeeding generations of Darwinists, culture was the means by which
7710: humans adapted to their environment. Great art and architecture,
7711: literature, music, systems of morality, and all the other professed
7712: attributes of ‘civilized’ life might be fine and pleasurable things to
7713: contemplate, but could be explained scientifically in terms of their
7714: attributes as part of a functioning cultural system. Victorian notions
7715: of morality were undercut by such a theory, emphasizing as it did that
7716: emotional or sentimental phenomena as ‘innocent’ as a maiden’s blush
7717: were sexual in origin.
7718: 
7719: Darwinism also leads to a highly sceptical view of any purpose, mission
7720: or teleology to human history. Such a view might sound strange, because
7721: in the nineteenth century Darwinism was harnessed to Herbert Spencer’s
7722: and Karl Marx’s philosophies of a definite human progress. It also
7723: sounds strange because the term ‘evolution’ is often associated in
7724: popular discourse with just such a teleology. However, a powerful
7725: implication of Darwin’s theory is that chance – random variation, the
7726: contingent combination of different inherited characteristics – plays a
7727: very large part in the world. In cultural evolution, and in humanistic
7728: study generally, the world has a purpose. In Darwinian evolution, by
7729: contrast, ‘the nature of genes is to make copies of themselves not with
7730: any grand plan or purpose in mind but simply because that is what they
7731: do’ (Cartwright 2000: 53).
7732: 
7733: All these points may sound quite obvious to a twenty-first-century
7734: generation for whom Darwinist ideas are apparently very familiar.
7735: However, I invite the student to think about them very carefully. One of
7736: the characteristics of Darwinist principles, as Michael LePage hints, is
7737: that people profess to accept them, but their implications are not
7738: always thought through in practice: ‘today, more than a century after
7739: Darwin’s death, we still have not come to terms with its mind-boggling
7740: implications’ (Dennett 1995: 19). This perceived failure to roll-out the
7741: implications of Darwin’s dangerous idea is especially apparent when the
7742: student turns away from the ‘natural world’ towards the study of human
7743: beings. Darwinists in the human sciences often complain that their
7744: colleagues and students continue to think of the human world in
7745: humanistic and Romantic ways long after these notions have, in their
7746: view, been utterly discredited.
7747: 
7748: Cultural Ecology
7749: 
7750: Darwinist ideas were based around a small group of key concepts,
7751: including variation, drift, adaptation, and natural selection. The
7752: central argument of Darwinist applications to the human sciences is very
7753: simple. Each of these ideas can be argued to apply not simply to the
7754: natural world, but to human and cultural life also. In this view, human
7755: and cultural life is part of that natural world.
7756: 
7757: Darwinist archaeology, then, is part of a wider movement in the human
7758: sciences, a movement which makes the claim that Darwinist principles are
7759: applicable not merely to biology, but to human culture also. If this
7760: claim is correct, then all or part of the archaeological record can be
7761: explained with reference to evolutionary ideas such as variation,
7762: adaptation and natural selection.
7763: 
7764: What is the evidence for this claim? First, variation can be seen in
7765: culture as well as in the natural world. Variation can be seen on many
7766: different levels and in different ways, from the zigging and zagging on
7767: pottery decoration, to variation between individuals and small groups,
7768: to variation between whole cultural groups however defined.
7769: 
7770: In biology, variation is argued to be random: it proceeds through
7771: genetic mutation, though as we have observed, the operation of genes was
7772: not understood in Darwin’s time. To take a classic and over-simplified
7773: example, why do giraffes have long necks? When giraffes reproduce and
7774: the result is variation in neck length between offspring, there is no
7775: intentionality behind this. Giraffes do not get longer and longer necks
7776: because they desire or want longer necks. Nor do giraffes act in a
7777: conscious or purposive way, for example by stretching and stretching in
7778: an attempt to get them. Rather, the variation is random. What happens is
7779: that natural selection acts upon the variation between offspring of
7780: giraffes. Giraffes who happen to have inherited longer necks from their
7781: parents find it easier to reach the leaves in higher branches of trees.
7782: They are better nourished, and stand a better chance of reproducing and
7783: passing on their genes to their offspring. Over time, then, giraffes
7784: with longer necks will come to numerically predominate.
7785: 
7786: In human culture, the causes of variation are more complex. They might
7787: be argued to be ‘random’ in some cases. For example, what has been
7788: termed ‘stylistic drift’ has been argued to occur when decorative
7789: features such as zigging versus zagging on pottery has no obvious or
7790: direct adaptive benefit. (It may well have an indirect adaptive
7791: consequence, however, for example by its function as a stylistic
7792: expression of group cohesion). However, much human behaviour is
7793: undeniably purposive or intentional. People in the past chose to build
7794: houses of a certain form, chose to plant particular crops, or chose to
7795: dress in a certain way. It is very difficult to see the patterns these
7796: choices produced as the result of ‘random mutation’. Rather, the choices
7797: were in part purposive – choice of what crops to plant or form of houses
7798: to build was informed by an appreciation of what strategy was likely to
7799: be successful (thus people do not choose to build igloos in central
7800: Africa or to attempt to grow yams in the Arctic).
7801: 
7802: Darwinists argue, however, that evolutionary principles can act on any
7803: variation regardless of whether it is ‘random’. Indeed, human intention
7804: and the cultural choices that humans make can mean that many
7805: evolutionary ‘dead ends’ are avoided. A giraffe with a short neck is the
7806: simple recipient of sheer bad luck, and there is nothing the poor animal
7807: can do about it. A human individual or group, however, can choose this
7808: subsistence strategy over that one; can discover and utilize new sources
7809: of food; can make the choice to move to a different area with a
7810: different or more abundant set of natural resources. In practice, very
7811: few human individuals or groups consciously choose a strategy that they
7812: believe or know to be a poor one – though examples can be argued. For
7813: example, one explanation for the failure of the Scandinavian settlement
7814: of Newfoundland in the early Middle Ages is that they attempted to
7815: persist with subsistence strategies more appropriate to their homeland,
7816: rather than follow the more ‘rational’ choice of adopting the strategies
7817: of their Inuit neighbours.
7818: 
7819: The study of human adaptation to the environment has been termed
7820: cultural ecology or human behavioural ecology. Cultural ecology is the
7821: belief that societies will be more or less adapted to their material
7822: environment, and therefore that the characteristics of those societies
7823: can be explained in terms of such adaptation. A powerful argument in
7824: much of ‘cultural ecology’ is the observation derived from ethnography
7825: that humans usually have a deep and sophisticated understanding of their
7826: environment, and will modify their behaviour to adapt to changes in that
7827: environment.
7828: 
7829: It may be the case that much human behaviour has unintended consequences
7830: for survival and reproduction. The reasons why human groups change their
7831: behaviour might be complex, and ostensibly governed by intentions
7832: unrelated to adaptation and reproduction – building temples to please
7833: the gods, or burying their dead with elaborate grave-goods for the
7834: afterlife. Nevertheless, cultural ecologists argue that much behaviour
7835: that has no obvious or immediate significance in facilitating
7836: reproductive success nevertheless does have consequences when looked at
7837: in detail and depth.
7838: 
7839: Much cultural ecology and cultural materialist anthropology concerns
7840: itself with taking cultural practices that appear ‘odd’ to Western eyes,
7841: or at least not obviously adaptive or utilitarian, and showing how such
7842: behaviour does have adaptive consequences and/or can be explained using
7843: an adaptive model. Many of its adherents are anthropologists working
7844: with modern societies. Examples of ideas from cultural ecology that have
7845: been taken up and used by archaeologists are site catchment analysis,
7846: optimal foraging theory, risk and seasonality.
7847: 
7848: Site catchment analysis is a body of techniques based on the mapping of
7849: resources around a site. It is assumed that people will exploit the
7850: landscape ‘rationally’, and they will utilize resources in such a way as
7851: to maximize returns. The technique, then, consists of drawing a line
7852: around a site’s location and looking at the resources within two, three,
7853: four hours’ walk of the site.
7854: 
7855: What constitutes the most adaptive strategy in the long term, however,
7856: is not simply a question of how to exploit the most resources; it also
7857: involves issues of risk and seasonality. Interest in ‘risk’ is based on
7858: the observation that it is not so much the overall productivity of an
7859: environment that is important for understanding human adaptations, but
7860: the degree of risk within it. Neolithic farming communities, for
7861: example, may be worried not so much about producing more food as such,
7862: but about what might happen during the odd ‘bad year’ of disaster and
7863: famine. It is no use a village producing enough food to support 500
7864: people nine years out of ten if there is only food to support 100 people
7865: in the tenth, famine year.
7866: 
7867: Many economic, social and even religious strategies, then, can be
7868: understood as risk minimization. Extended networks of kin, trading
7869: contacts, food storage: all these phenomena have been interpreted by
7870: anthropologists and archaeologists as ways to mitigate the risk of
7871: subsistence crisis. If food is short one year, one can call on one’s
7872: distant cousins; or on trade partners who owe you obligations; or on the
7873: tribe to whom you gave marriage partners. Thus, elaborate social
7874: networks such as those of Australian Aborigines or Inuit Native
7875: Americans can be explained as adaptive responses to ‘highrisk’
7876: environments such as the Australian desert or the Arctic tundra.
7877: 
7878: ‘Seasonality’ refers to an interest in how human groups survive through
7879: the year. There may be plenty of resources in an environment for most of
7880: the year, but this is of no use if people will starve 2 months out of
7881: 12. Peter Rowley-Conwy looked at the Ertebølle culture of Denmark and
7882: suggested that the collection of oysters, though not important in terms
7883: of overall caloric intake, was absolutely critical at a certain time of
7884: year when few other resources were available. This observation in turn
7885: helped RowleyConwy explain site location and other aspects of Ertebølle
7886: behaviour.
7887: 
7888: Cultural ecology, then, explores the ways in which human culture is
7889: ‘adaptive’ at the level of the cultural group. There is, however, a
7890: second set of approaches, which examine selection, variation and
7891: adaptation at different levels – of genetic transmission between
7892: individuals.
7893:
