7894: Genes and Memes
7895: 
7896: One application of Darwinism to human culture is to argue that much
7897: human behaviour is not determined by ‘culture’ at all, but rather is
7898: genetically programmed or determined. Sociobiology is a form of theory
7899: that attempts to explain features of human behaviour as being the result
7900: of genetic/biological rather than social, non-genetic causes. It
7901: suggests that certain behaviours are the result of genetic propensities
7902: that were adaptive at some point in the past. Sociobiology is an
7903: exceptionally controversial theory of culture, since the stress on
7904: particular propensities for behaviour (aggression, group loyalty,
7905: altruism) being rooted in genes is often interpreted by its opponents as
7906: being racist or sexist. For example, some sociobiologists argue that it
7907: makes adaptive sense for males to spread their genes around widely and
7908: reproduce as much as possible; female primates, by contrast, can only
7909: have a limited number of children, and need a stable partner for
7910: protection against predators and other environmental dangers. Therefore,
7911: it is argued, women in modern Western society are genetically
7912: predisposed to stable relationships, whereas men are genetically
7913: predisposed to sleep with as many sexual partners as possible. Opponents
7914: of sociobiology see this as a thinly-disguised attempt at an ideological
7915: justification for male infidelity – ‘I couldn’t help it, dear, it’s in
7916: my genes’. Sociobiology is seen by many, including many Darwinists, as
7917: excessively simplistic in its approach; it is very difficult to point to
7918: convincing or sustained case studies that apply sociobiology to the
7919: archaeological record.
7920: 
7921: A more subtle way to think about the genetic basis of human behaviour is
7922: proposed by evolutionary psychology. Evolutionary psychologists develop
7923: explanations of human cognitive traits as the products of past processes
7924: of adaptation and natural selection. We saw in chapter 6 that
7925: evolutionary psychology was an important part of cognitive archaeology,
7926: in that is proposes evolutionary explanations for the development of
7927: language, of symbolic thought, and so on. Much of this work was
7928: prefigured by Darwin himself, who in his later years looked at the
7929: expression of emotion in humans, relating it to patterns of behaviour
7930: that he hypothesized had been adaptive in the past. Evolutionary
7931: psychology tends to have a more complex view of the relationship between
7932: genetic make-up and human culture, a view taken up by co-evolutionary
7933: theory (see below).
7934: 
7935: A subtle way to avoid some of the conceptual issues raised by
7936: sociobiology and evolutionary psychology is to think more carefully
7937: about the unit of selection. In biology, the unit of selection can
7938: either be the gene, or the individual organism; there is a complex and
7939: ongoing debate in biology over which of these is the appropriate unit of
7940: analysis. Richard Dawkins’ famous book ‘The Selfish Gene’ was arguably
7941: mistitled; genes are not selfish, they are just genes; they do what they
7942: do, that is, replicate themselves. However, his point was that it is
7943: genes which replicate and which are or are not successful in doing so,
7944: not individual organisms.
7945: 
7946: Darwinists studying human culture have explored the implications of
7947: Dawkins’ observation. Dawkins proposed that while biological
7948: transmission occurs genetically, human culture consists of memes. A meme
7949: can be defined as any cultural unit that is passed from one person to
7950: another by non-genetic means. A meme can be an idea, a particular
7951: behaviour, or a cultural value; and it can be passed from one individual
7952: to another by imitation, teaching or other forms of cultural
7953: transmission. Memes are in this view like genes, in that they carry
7954: code. However, the mode of transmission of behaviour here is memetic,
7955: rather than genetic. Memetic transmission can be analogous to genes, or
7956: it can be very different: thus, while genetic variability is the result
7957: of ‘copying errors’, cultural behaviour may change not just through
7958: ‘drift’ but also through intentional behaviour, for example innovation
7959: or adoption of different cultural practices. As a result, many of the
7960: problems of sociobiology, both conceptual and ethical, are avoided.
7961: 
7962: The idea of memes is attractive to Darwinian archaeologists because it
7963: gets around a key problem. Sociobiology asserts the primacy of genetic
7964: transmission of behaviour; but clearly, in human history, there have
7965: been cultural practices that have been very successful and have
7966: flourished, though they have not necessarily facilitated the
7967: reproduction of particular individuals.
7968: 
7969: Co-Evolutionary Theory
7970: 
7971: A particular form of Darwinist thought that has proven popular among
7972: archaeologists is ‘co-evolutionary’ or ‘dual inheritance’ theory.
7973: Co-evolutionary theory does not claim to reduce ‘culture’ to biology or
7974: vice versa. For Richerson and Boyd, culture is part of biology, but not
7975: reducible to it:
7976: 
7977: Much evidence suggests that we have an evolved psychology that shapes
7978: what we learn and how we think, and that this in turn influences the
7979: kind of beliefs and attitudes that spread and persist. Theories that
7980: ignore these connections cannot adequately account for much of human
7981: behavior. At the same time, culture and cultural change cannot be
7982: understood solely in terms of innate psychology. Culture affects the
7983: success and survival of individuals and groups; as a result, some
7984: cultural variants spread and others diminish, leading to evolutionary
7985: processes that are every bit as real and important as those that shape
7986: genetic variation. These culturally evolved environments then affect
7987: which genes are favored by natural selection. Over the evolutionary long
7988: haul, culture has shaped our innate psychology as much as the other way
7989: around (Richerson and Boyd 2005: 4)
7990: 
7991: One of the strengths of this argument is that it moves beyond rather
7992: sterile ‘nature’ versus ‘nurture’ arguments, which Richerson and Boyd
7993: regard as misguided. On the one hand, humanists and others have often
7994: taken a ‘superorganic’ view of culture, insisting that the mind is a
7995: ‘blank slate’ upon which different cultural systems are more or less
7996: free to write what they like. On the other, some Darwinists have tended
7997: towards a naïve sociobiological position in which everything that we
7998: think and do is genetically determined. Richerson and Boyd reject this
7999: opposition; culture for them, is obviously implicated in biology, but
8000: genes act as a recipe, not a determining code.
8001: 
8002: One of the powerful implications of this argument is that Richerson and
8003: Boyd do not divorce or oppose culture to biology, but they do affirm
8004: that culture changes evolutionary processes qualitatively: ‘Culture is
8005: interesting and important because its evolutionary behavior is
8006: distinctly different from that of genes.’ Part of culture can be seen as
8007: memetic, that is operating in a manner analogous to genes, but memetic
8008: processes are not the only cultural processes at work.
8009: 
8010: Richerson and Boyd’s views are controversial, but they do open up an
8011: intellectual space which moves beyond sterile nature/nurture debates,
8012: and enables one to explore the interaction of culture and biology
8013: without having to sign up to a reductionist position. It is striking
8014: that recent Darwinist archaeologies of a more eclectic and theoretically
8015: flexible brand such as those emanating from the UCL Centre for the
8016: Evolution of Cultural Diversity have drawn heavily on Richerson and
8017: Boyd’s work.
8018: 
8019: Selectionist Archaeology
8020: 
8021: A stronger and more fundamental position of is that of the ‘selectionist
8022: archaeology’ of Robert Dunnell and his students. Selectionist
8023: archaeology starts from a very strong assertion of archaeology as a
8024: science, and a dogmatic rejection of other forms of archaeological
8025: enquiry as basically misconceived. For example, the entire project of
8026: cultural evolution is dismissed as ‘unscientific’ and having nothing to
8027: do with biological models. Dunnell separates two very different strands
8028: of nineteenth-century thought: Spencer’s stress on social totalities
8029: leading to cultural evolution; and the adaptationist arguments of
8030: Darwin. Only the latter, he claims, is ‘scientific’. Cultural evolution
8031: is unscientific because it substitutes cause/intention for a scientific
8032: framework: ‘it employs different notions of cause, lacks theory in the
8033: usual sense of the word, and employs an entirely different research
8034: strategy’ (Dunnell 1989: 38). Dunnell also rejects sociobiology, on the
8035: grounds that most transmission of traits is cultural rather than
8036: genetic.
8037: 
8038: Instead of positing the individual as the unit of selection,
8039: selectionist archaeology focuses on the ‘phenotype’. Phenotypes are
8040: defined as the physical and behavioural elements of organisms;
8041: ‘artefacts are the hard parts of the behavioural segment of phenotypes’,
8042: like shells or nests for other species. Selectionist archaeology, then,
8043: focuses on the success or failure of phenotypes in replicating. Much of
8044: selectionist archaeology has focused on a very sharp distinction between
8045: style and function. It is proposed that functionally advantageous traits
8046: will be selected for. However, stylistic traits will be subject to
8047: ‘drift’.
8048: 
8049: Selectionist archaeology makes two interesting intellectual alliances.
8050: First, in its stress on understanding the archaeological record rather
8051: than on the human actions and processes, and also in its rejection of
8052: cultural evolutionary models, it ends up in a similar position to much
8053: traditional culture history. Second, as Dunnell has pointed out, it
8054: reverts to a version of evolutionary theory earlier than that of Darwin.
8055: Recall the example of the giraffe’s long neck discussed above. An
8056: earlier generation of biologists, particularly the eighteenth-century
8057: thinker Lamarck, had posited that giraffes get their longer necks
8058: through some kind of conscious or unconscious striving on their part.
8059: Selectionist archaeology claims to revert to this Lamarckian rather than
8060: Darwinian model, in that artefacts or phenotypes are the ‘hard’ or
8061: ‘fossil’ part of human culture. The programme of selectionist
8062: archaeology has been especially popular in North America. Conversely, in
8063: other parts of the world, scholars have tended to see it as resting on a
8064: misunderstanding of Darwinist principles; it is striking, for example,
8065: that Shennan’s work makes very little reference to it.
8066: 
8067: Selectionist archaeology has few case studies worked through in detail
8068: to back it up, in part, I think, because its challenge to established
8069: modes of archaeological enquiry is so fundamental and far-reaching. If
8070: Dunnell is correct, then we must abandon much of traditional
8071: archaeology, including what he terms ‘model descriptions’ of phases,
8072: cultures and periods, as well as ‘behavioural reconstruction’. Bettinger
8073: and Eerkens note ‘an unfortunate parallel between evolutionary
8074: archaeology and the weather: everyone talks about it but no one does
8075: anything about it’ (1997: 177). Nevertheless, models such as these have
8076: attracted a committed following in the last few decades, particularly in
8077: North American archaeology.
8078: 
8079: Case Study: Art, Handaxes, Population and Innovation in the Palaeolithic
8080: 
8081: A range of examples of how Darwinist ideas have affected archaeological
8082: interpretations can be taken from human origins research. The first is
8083: Steven Mithen’s explanation of the archaeological record of the
8084: Palaeolithic, including cave art and handaxes. Mithen suggests that the
8085: creativity of cave art is an adaptive response to an environment full of
8086: risk and fluctuation, in which there was often plenty of game around but
8087: in which sharp fluctuations could occur. He draws attention to the
8088: content of the art – images of animals that he interprets as encoding
8089: valuable information for hunters – and its distribution – in an area
8090: rich in game and possibly dense in population at this period.
8091: 
8092: Mithen suggests that the art relates to critical moments in changing
8093: hunting strategies when changes in the environment forced hunters to
8094: adapt by turning from group activities to solitary hunting. The art,
8095: then, helped prepare hunters for such changes. Mithen suggests that
8096: hunters were aware that troughs in reindeer/red deer populations would
8097: occur, but were unsure when.
8098: 
8099: What is striking about Mithen’s approach is its use of different ideas.
8100: He uses ideas of adaptation to a changing environment to explain a
8101: phenomenon – art – not normally regarded as amenable to an adaptive
8102: approach. Mithen insists that ‘creativity’ and ‘adaptation’ are not
8103: mutually exclusive; indeed, he comments:
8104: 
8105: I am left wondering about a possible connection between the creative act
8106: of producing images and the creative thoughts about hunting behaviour
8107: that such images helped create. I feel that these must have fed off each
8108: other in a spiral of creativity leaving us today with the splendours of
8109: Palaeolithic art and enabling the Palaeolithic hunters to adapt to their
8110: uncertain world. (Mithen 1990: 13)
8111: 
8112: Mithen’s explanation here is loosely inspired by cultural ecology, but
8113: his more recent interpretations of handaxes as ‘products of sexual
8114: selection’ (Kohn and Mithen 1999, 518), in collaboration with Kohn, is
8115: more directly and fundamentally derived from Darwinian ideas. Handaxes
8116: are one of the most common, and classic, artefacts of the Acheulian
8117: (figure 10.4); one of their most striking features is that their form
8118: remains extremely stable over a million years. Kohn and Mithen observe
8119: that many of these handaxes are very carefully produced, in a way that
8120: goes beyond what is needed for utilitarian purposes. In particular,
8121: forms of handaxe are carefully designed to be as symmetrical as
8122: possible. Kohn and Mithen draw on Darwinian models of sexual selection
8123: theory, which stress how reproduction is the outcome in part of female
8124: sexual selection, and which explores why females choose certain traits
8125: in the male over others. Sexual selection theorists have pointed out
8126: that symmetry, in biology, is an indicator of good genes, and is one
8127: criterion of ‘mate selection’. They are arguing for a bias on the part
8128: of females towards symmetry, and that symmetrical handaxes would be read
8129: at some level as an indicator of good genes, or more broadly in terms of
8130: the male’s success at acquiring resources, health, and social and
8131: cognitive awareness. Males, then, learnt to produce particularly
8132: impressive and symmetrical handaxes for the purpose of attracting mates.
8133: 
8134: Figure 10.4 Handaxes from Cuxton, southern England. Photographs courtesy
8135: of Francis Wenban-smith
8136: 
8137: [c10_image004.jpg]
8138: 
8139: This theory is a classic example of Darwinist ideas in archaeology, even
8140: down to the way like so many Darwinian arguments it has been popularized
8141: (and viewed with humour, by both proponents and detractors) as ‘sexy
8142: handaxe theory’). It can be criticized from both within and without a
8143: Darwinian perspective, for example on evidential grounds: right or
8144: wrong, there are quite a number of very wide and bold inferential leaps
8145: between observed patterns in the archaeological record (symmetry in
8146: handaxes) and explanatory theories (female sexual selection). For
8147: example, there is no hard evidence that males rather than females were
8148: producing handaxes, or that there was competition between males for
8149: females as mates; both these features may be suggested by comparative
8150: studies of primates, but remain unproven.
8151: 
8152: A third example is Stephen Shennan’s study of cultural innovation and
8153: the emergence of modern human culture after 60,000 bp. Shennan is
8154: interested in the relationship between population size on the one hand
8155: and cultural innovation on the other. He takes a co-evolutionary
8156: approach to understanding cultural transmission and innovation, and
8157: discusses the means of transmission and change in craft traditions, most
8158: obviously stone tool production. Shennan develops a complex mathematical
8159: simulation which demonstrates that the larger the population size, the
8160: more successful innovation is likely to be:
8161: 
8162: When cultural innovation processes take place and the results are passed
8163: on by a combination of vertical and oblique transmission, larger
8164: populations have a very major advantage over smaller ones. Quite simply,
8165: members of larger populations are on average both biologically fitter
8166: and more attractive as models for imitation, by virtue of the fact that
8167: the deleterious sampling effects present in small populations decline as
8168: population sizes increase. When populations are small, innovations which
8169: are less beneficial reproductively and less attractive to imitate are
8170: more likely to be maintained within them. (Shennan 2001: 12).
8171: 
8172: Shennan concludes, therefore, that a key to understanding the increased
8173: rate of change in human culture after 60,000 bp is the relatively rapid
8174: increase in population size after this date, compared with very low
8175: population levels before that date: ‘demographic fluctuations may be the
8176: single most important factor in explaining how and why the emergence of
8177: modern human culture occurred when it did’ (Shennan 2001: 15) – and
8178: conversely, Shennan adds, low population levels may explain why earlier
8179: handaxes changed so very little over time.
8180: 
8181: Darwinian Archaeologies: Criticisms
8182: 
8183: Criticisms of Darwinist archaeologies and their inspirations in
8184: Darwinian applications to the human sciences are difficult to list in a
8185: straightforward fashion. As we have seen, the school of thought itself
8186: is a varied and complex beast. Criticisms directed, for example, at
8187: cultural ecology are quite misdirected at certain forms of biological
8188: evolution or other programmes such as optimal foraging theory. As
8189: Darwinian models of evolution vary in what they consider to be the
8190: appropriate unit of analysis (individual, culture, phenotype), and as
8191: proponents are quite insistent that these variations are fundamental
8192: scientific differences rather than changes in nuance or relative
8193: emphasis, critique of one model is not necessarily applicable to others.
8194: 
8195: Shanks and Tilley come closest to a complete rejection of such models.
8196: They reject any kind of evolutionary or ecological theory on grounds
8197: that are basically similar to their rejection of systems thinking.
8198: Concepts such as time budgeting, risk and cost-benefit analysis are,
8199: Shanks and Tilley argue, ideas that are derived from modern economics:
8200: that is, the study of modern capitalist societies. If, as Shanks and
8201: Tilley suggest, people in the past had systems of cultural values that
8202: were other than those of modern capitalists, it follows that such
8203: concepts are inapplicable to them. In fact, we can go further: if
8204: cultural ecologists claim that such analyses hold any truth, then they
8205: are in fact claiming that capitalist values are true in all times and
8206: all places. Cultural ecology is therefore, for Shanks and Tilley,
8207: ideological – as we discussed in chapter 6, an ideology is a system of
8208: beliefs that makes certain values appear universal, normal and natural.
8209: 
8210: Cultural ecologists counter that, as we look at other cultures, their
8211: beliefs are indeed more or less ‘rational’. Modern ethnographic studies,
8212: they claim, show that all or most cultures do in fact have a ‘rational’
8213: view of their environment, and that therefore we can apply modern
8214: ‘rational’ concepts such as time-budgeting and risk.
8215: 
8216: This is an unresolved argument, but it is worth pointing out its roots
8217: in economic anthropology. In the 1960s and 1970s, many anthropologists
8218: working on the economics of modern non-Western societies claimed that
8219: all such societies made ‘rational’ economic choices that could be
8220: understood using the ideas and vocabulary of Western economists –
8221: marginal cost, energy flows, risk, etc. This was called the formalist
8222: school of economic anthropology. Formalists were opposed by
8223: substantivists, who argued that Western ideas were inappropriate for the
8224: study of the economic choices of other cultures. The ‘formalist versus
8225: substantivist debate’ has lost its heat more recently, but it continues
8226: to surface from time to time in debates over the claims of cultural
8227: ecology.
8228: 
8229: A related attack on evolution and ecology has come via social
8230: constructivism, the social critique of science that we discussed in
8231: chapter 3. If a culture is indeed adapted to an external environment, it
8232: is argued, what was that environment? The resources and risks in that
8233: environment are delineated by the techniques of Western Science. But if
8234: those techniques are themselves socially constructed, they cannot claim
8235: to be a ‘neutral’ or ‘objective’ mapping of the resources in that
8236: environment. A different way of making the same objection is via an
8237: attack on essentialism: that is, if one deconstructs the idea that the
8238: body has certain ‘natural’ or ‘biologically endowed’ needs and desires,
8239: then the landscape cannot be interpreted as a set of resources that may
8240: fulfil those needs and desires.
8241: 
8242: Constructivists tend to make very strong critiques on the basis that
8243: Darwinism is an unacceptably reductionist view of human behaviour, a
8244: crude take-over attempt by biologists of the human sciences. Darwinists
8245: respond that constructivism is an anti-science agenda, a crude take-over
8246: attempt by the human sciences of biology and the natural sciences. Who
8247: is right? Read the literature, and then make up your own mind.
8248: 
8249: Conclusion
8250: 
8251: The application of ideas of Darwinian evolution to archaeology today
8252: continues to be one of the most controversial topics in archaeological
8253: theory. No serious scholar doubts the theory of natural selection, any
8254: more than they doubt the theory of gravity. However, there remains a
8255: major underlying tension in the way archaeologists think about human
8256: culture, and specifically the question of whether and how far Darwinist
8257: models of descent with modification and natural selection are useful or
8258: productive ways of understanding human behaviour generally, or the
8259: archaeological record in particular.
8260: 
8261: Like many theories, there is a great deal of bad Darwinism around, most
8262: frequently to be found in the popular press. Studies such as the
8263: following are of dubious origin and methodology, and are frequently
8264: quoted by opponents as exemplars of bad science:
8265: 
8266: Shoppers are using instincts they learnt from their Neanderthal
8267: ancestors, researchers have found.
8268: 
8269: Dr David Holmes, of Manchester Metropolitan University, said skills that
8270: were learnt as cavemen and women were now being used in shops. He said:
8271: ‘Gatherers sifted the useful from things that offered them no
8272: sustenance, warmth or comfort with a skill that would eventually lead to
8273: comfortable shopping malls and credit cards. In our evolutionary past,
8274: we gathered in caves with fires at the entrance. We repeat this in warm
8275: shopping centres where we can flit from store to store without braving
8276: the icy winds.’
8277: 
8278: The study was commissioned by Manchester Arndale shopping centre … (Ben
8279: Leach, Daily Telegraph, 25.02.2009)
8280: 
8281: In my view, there is a major underlying tension in the way that
8282: archaeologists approach the study of the past. As with cultural
8283: evolution, supporters, and critics, of such ideas, do not always do what
8284: they say they do. Darwinian archaeology is often (though not always)
8285: associated with a strong belief in Science, and a resistance to what are
8286: perceived as social or political agendas to deconstruct or to devalue
8287: Science. However, as we saw with the sexy handaxe theory, there are
8288: arguably as many evidential gaps in Darwinist explanations of the
8289: archaeological record as there are in parallel and competing
8290: interpretive theories. This is not to say that Darwinist applications
8291: are necessarily wrong, but it is to make a plea that it give up the
8292: strong rhetorical statements it makes to be a superior, or indeed the
8293: only, way of constructing scholarly knowledge of the past.
8294: 
8295: Darwinism is often presented as a single way of knowing, a theory that
8296: is very simple in its essentials and provides a powerful key to
8297: explaining human behaviour. Its proponents characteristically make the
8298: claim that theirs is not just another way of thinking about the past,
8299: but a better, even the only, scientific way of understanding the
8300: archaeological record. Darwinism promises to deliver a methodologically
8301: complex and difficult, but theoretically simple and unitary, way of
8302: thinking about the archaeological record. Its language and rhetoric is
8303: often uncompromising, even fundamentalist. For more dogmatic Darwinists,
8304: their way is the only way, both much more difficult and at the same time
8305: much more valuable than the fluffy humanism of lesser colleagues: ‘to
8306: gain an appreciation of just how different Darwinism is from other
8307: theories about the natural world, one cannot simply read a few books or
8308: articles and come away with the requisite understanding. Rather one
8309: needs to wade through myriad epistemological issues’ (O’Brien and Lyman
8310: 2000: 72).
8311: 
8312: However, what Darwinian applications to the human sciences actually
8313: deliver, when their results are examined critically and in detail, is a
8314: series of themes that are just as slippery and multifaceted as the most
8315: ‘nuanced’ set of interpretive archaeologies. What is different is the
8316: rhetorical style taken, of confrontation rather than accommodation; the
8317: interpretive ‘difference in emphasis’ or ‘chosen interpretive turn’ is
8318: the Darwinist’s ‘fundamental misunderstanding’ or just being ‘wrong’.
8319: 
8320: The student seeking to understand Darwinian thought, and to apply it to
8321: the archaeological record, is rapidly disillusioned by the promise of a
8322: definite method or theory. He or she is presented with difficult choices
8323: after his or her first introductory readings. Darwinist archaeology is
8324: not, in practice, a single way of knowing, but rather a series of
8325: divergent routes, the choice of which will lead to ritual denunciation
8326: from the spurned parties. What is the unit of selection – the
8327: social/cultural group, the population, the individual, the gene or the
8328: meme? How is the ‘environment’ defined? Is a strictly Darwinian model
8329: appropriate, or a Lamarckian model, à la Dunnell? Within this forest of
8330: arguments and choice, a path can be traced that is not necessarily
8331: incommensurate or incompatible with interpretive models. Darwin, after
8332: all, ‘is sometimes said to have biologized human culture, but he is more
8333: accurately accused of culturizing biology’ (Richerson and Boyd 2005:
8334: 16).
8335: 
8336: An important and exciting body of work that I have alluded to briefly in
8337: this chapter is that of placing Darwin in his nineteenth-century
8338: context, and his cultural impact and role as an intellectual in his
8339: time. The supreme irony is that the man whose work initiated a
8340: scientific dismantling of much of the essentialism of the study of
8341: ‘human culture’, and has led to such unabashed claims of the superiority
8342: of ‘science’ over other forms of knowledge, has become a cultural icon,
8343: in ways that (in my view) are very difficult to explain using a
8344: Darwinist framework. Darwin is now a megastar. He is one of the very few
8345: scientists (along with Albert Einstein) whose face is instantly
8346: recognizable (figure 10.1); he appears on the British ten pound note, on
8347: arte-facts such as mugs and bumper stickers, and was voted the Fourth
8348: Greatest Briton in a BBC TV poll (behind Winston Churchill, Isambard
8349: Kingdom Brunel and Lady Diana, but beating William Shakespeare into an
8350: ignominious fifth place).
8351: 
8352: What is needed, then, is a renunciation of loud position statements
8353: proclaiming the inherent superiority of the Darwinist project, or
8354: conversely blanket condemnations of its moral or political iniquity.
8355: Rather, future decades should see a very difficult project demanding
8356: hard theoretical work on both sides, namely an understanding of the
8357: evolution of human culture and society that does not seek to reduce
8358: culture to biology, but which does seek to grasp the very subtle and
8359: complex relationship of different elements of human behaviour. In my
8360: view, it is striking that the most impressive and convincing
8361: applications of Darwinist ideas have done exactly this. For example, in
8362: Origins and Revolutions (2007), Clive Gamble has used ideas derived from
8363: the history of brain evolution alongside interpretive ideas of
8364: landscape, personhood and materiality.
8365: 
8366: This is a massive and very difficult undertaking, spanning as it does
8367: the concerns of the humanities and human sciences as much as it is a
8368: project internal to archaeology. Indeed, its difficulty is such that
8369: simple name-calling of other schools as ‘unscientific’, or conversely as
8370: ‘reductionist’, is often preferred to engaging in such a project. But
8371: however difficult it is, I would argue that such a project is not ‘good’
8372: or ‘bad’ – it is simply necessary.
8373: 
8374: 11
8375: 
8376: Archaeology and History
8377: 
8378: For many in North America, archaeology is seen as a subfield of
8379: anthropology, or at the very least the two disciplines are seen as
8380: closely linked. In Europe, a straw poll of the views of most
8381: archaeologists would suggest that the sister discipline of archaeology
8382: is history.
8383: 
8384: In part, this difference reflects the different subject matter in North
8385: America and Europe. The archaeology of North America is largely
8386: prehistoric until the first colonies of the fifteenth, sixteenth and
8387: seventeenth centuries ad. There is a flourishing and vibrant ‘historical
8388: archaeology’ of the period of the American colonies and of eighteenth-
8389: and nineteenth- century North America, but numerically it is very much a
8390: minority pursuit within Americanist archaeology. The archaeologists
8391: based in North American Classics departments are sometimes at the
8392: forefront of theoretical debates, but not always. In contrast, the
8393: ‘historical horizon’ is much earlier in Europe. Europeanists have
8394: historical texts that bear on the pre-Roman Iron Age; archaeologists
8395: specializing in this or later periods form over half the teaching staff
8396: in most British universities, and at a rough estimate over half the
8397: excavations in Europe take place on Classical or post-Roman sites. All
8398: these archaeologists deal with historical texts, and therefore with
8399: university colleagues in Classics or History departments, in some form.
8400: The full story of this divergence between Britain and North America in
8401: terms of disciplinary allies, however, goes a little deeper than this.
8402: As we saw in chapter 2, this different disciplinary configuration led to
8403: the differential impact of the New Archaeology in Britain and America,
8404: and between prehistoric and Classical archaeology. It also led to the
8405: continuing flourishing and variety of ‘traditional’, culture-historical
8406: approaches in Britain and especially continental Europe.
8407: 
8408: In this chapter, I want to look at some of the different theoretical
8409: views taken on the relationship between archaeology and history. Before
8410: I can do this, however, we must look at different views of historical
8411: theory and practice. The theoretical positions of historians are as
8412: varied as those of archaeologists. To an extent, debates within
8413: historical theory run in parallel to those within archaeology.
8414: 
8415: Traditional History
8416: 
8417: Historians often like to trace their roots as a discipline back to the
8418: nineteenth century with figures such as the German historian Ranke.
8419: Ranke espoused what today would be seen as a narrow empiricism: his aim
8420: was to tell history wie es eigentlich gewesen , ‘as it really was’. He
8421: criticized grand theoretical schemes of human history and attempts to
8422: draw moral and political judgments from historical material. The task of
8423: the late nineteenth-century historian was simply to amass piles of
8424: facts, which, it was held, would speak for themselves.
8425: 
8426: Coupled with an empiricist and inductivist approach to the material went
8427: a stress on history as a narrative of political events. Traditional
8428: historians told a story. Traditional history books often have a
8429: beginning, a middle and an end to their plots. Indeed, it can be argued
8430: that while traditional historians protested (and protest) that they have
8431: no theory, in fact they follow literary rules of narrative and
8432: emplotment that have been ably characterized by the historical theorist
8433: Hayden White.
8434: 
8435: Traditional political history of this kind continues to be written to
8436: the present day. However, there has been a widening of historical
8437: thinking in several directions too wide-ranging to even detail here. One
8438: example of this widening of approach is the Annales school of French
8439: historians (named after their journal, Annales: Economies, Societies,
8440: Civilisations ) such as Marc Bloch, Fernand Braudel, Emmanuel Le Roy
8441: Ladurie and Jacques Le Goff. The Annales school has been much cited by
8442: archaeologists as a useful inspiration for archaeological thought, so it
8443: is worth reviewing its theories briefly.
8444: 
8445: The Annales School
8446: 
8447: First, Annales historians broadened their interests to include all
8448: aspects of past societies. They looked not just at the political
8449: machinations of the élite, but also at economy, society and cognition.
8450: In this sense the Annales school helped history become more
8451: anthropological and processual – that is, to understand total cultural
8452: systems rather than just tell stories about its political events.
8453: 
8454: Second, the Annales school broadened historical interest in the nature
8455: of time. Traditional historians tended to see time as no more than just
8456: a series of events such as battles, political treaties, births,
8457: coronations and deaths of monarchs. The task of the historian in this
8458: view was simply to describe these events as they happened one after
8459: another. The Annales school wanted to look at longer-term processes that
8460: underlay particular events. Underneath the ‘surface froth’ of battles
8461: and treaties, they saw economic, environmental and demographic trends
8462: and cycles. As discussed by Fernand Braudel, history moved at three
8463: scales or rhythms:
8464: 
8465: 1 that of events, l’histoire evenementielle;
8466: 
8467: 2 medium-term cycles such as economic boom and bust; and
8468: 
8469: 3 the very long term, ‘enduring structures’ or longue dureé (climatic
8470: changes, physical geography).
8471: 
8472: Note the similarity between the Annales notion of the long term and the
8473: archaeological concept of process as a trend underlying specific
8474: variability (discussed in chapter 2). Note also two areas of debate
8475: prompted by Annales thinking that have parallels within archaeological
8476: theory:
8477: 
8478: 1 Which (if any) of the three levels or scales of time is dominant? Each
8479: timescale might have its own logic; they might intersect at specific
8480: conjunctures. At times Braudel implied that the longue dureé dominated
8481: the other two levels; other Annales historians implied that the three
8482: levels interacted and that no one level was dominant.
8483: 
8484: 2 Are people’s thoughts important, or does the environment dominate?
8485: Some writers suggested that what they called ‘mentalité’ or mentalities
8486: could be just as much part of the longue dureé as climate or physical
8487: geography. For example, Jacques Le Goff talked of ‘the medieval mind’ as
8488: a mentality spanning five centuries that structured medieval people’s
8489: attitudes towards economic life and the natural world. So while Braudel
8490: tended to equate environment with the long term and to treat environment
8491: as a base-line, no such equation is logically necessary.
8492: 
8493: For example, in his book The Peasants of Languedoc Emmanuel Le Roy
8494: Ladurie looked at the lives of peasants in southern France. He used
8495: quantitative evidence, for example on the nutrition of peasants and of
8496: daily wage-rates; he used a loosely systemic model to understand why
8497: populations grew, stabilized and contracted at certain points; he
8498: emphasized the importance of ‘environmental’ variables such as climate.
8499: At the same time, his book Montaillou is one of the most famous
8500: reconstructions of peasant mentalities in historical literature. Le Roy
8501: Ladurie’s work, and Annales thinking in general, therefore incorporates
8502: many of the tensions between environment and society, short and long
8503: term, seen in archaeology and the human sciences in general.
8504: 
8505: The Linguistic Turn
8506: 
8507: In the decades after 1980, however, there has been a move taking
8508: historians still further away from traditional forms of historical
8509: discourse: this has been called the ‘linguistic turn’.
8510: 
8511: A good way to explain this shift is to look at the story of English
8512: ‘social history’. In the 1960s, very much in parallel with New
8513: Archaeology and New Geography, and influenced also by the Annales
8514: school, historians working within the Cambridge Group for the Study of
8515: Population and Social Structure turned to new areas outside traditional
8516: history. Some writers even termed this the ‘New Social History’. The New
8517: Social History sought answers to the dynamics of past societies through
8518: the use of statistics and looked at processes such as demographic cycles
8519: and fertility rates, using systemic jargon to understand the links
8520: between these. Cross-cultural analogies were used, for example between
8521: Europe before and during the Industrial Revolution and modern
8522: ‘developing’ societies.
8523: 
8524: But the New Social History rapidly found that key elements of the
8525: answers to the questions it was asking lay in ‘sentiment’ and
8526: ‘mentality’ – that is, in the realm of thoughts and ideologies. A good
8527: example is the New Social History’s attempt to understand changing
8528: patterns of marriage. Historians could not understand statistics of
8529: changing age of marriage, for example, without looking at changing
8530: attitudes and sentiments (for example, changing attitudes towards
8531: illegitimacy, attitudes towards patriarchy, or the rise of the modern
8532: notion of romantic love).
8533: 
8534: As a result, historians turned to other forms of evidence to explore
8535: people’s attitudes. These included such ‘non-statistical’ sources as
8536: conduct books prescribing manners and behaviour, court accounts of
8537: popular sentiments expressed at church, even literary and poetic
8538: descriptions of feeling and behaviour.
8539: 
8540: At the same time, historians became responsive to the argument that
8541: there was no one objective historical truth or ‘social reality’. If the
8542: sentiments underlying ‘marriage’ had to be explored, these sentiments
8543: obviously varied. There was no one correct answer to the question ‘What
8544: was marriage really like in seventeenth-century England?’; there were
8545: many different experiences of marriage, many possible interpretations of
8546: what people ‘really thought’ about marriage – different views of women,
8547: of men, of the élite, of the working classes. And these interpretations
8548: were unavoidably subjective – any historical account of marriage was
8549: written and was read within a contemporary society in which the idea of
8550: marriage seemed to be under grave threat or at the very least to be
8551: changing.
8552: 
8553: As a result, social historians saw the dissolution of objective facts to
8554: the extent that some historical theorists proclaimed the ‘end of social
8555: history’. Many argued that we could not have ‘social reality’ because
8556: ‘what was really going on’ in any given historical situation was open to
8557: multiple interpretations; people’s social lives were constructed within
8558: cultural understandings and beliefs. These beliefs, of course, were
8559: variable and could be ‘read’ in different ways, just as in chapter 7 we
8560: discussed different ‘readings’ of archaeological material. Instead of an
8561: objective society with a concrete existence, we had a more amorphous set
8562: of cultural beliefs. Behind ‘social reality’ lay cultural perception. So
8563: out of the New Social History was born … the New Cultural History. This
8564: move was paralleled within the Annales School itself; where an earlier
8565: generation had studied ‘mentalit é’ as a fixed and invariant deep
8566: structure, a later generation headed by Roger Chartier preferred to
8567: write more nuanced histories of particular cultural practices.
8568: 
8569: This excursion into historical theory is brief, potted and incomplete in
8570: the extreme. I want to make two points with it, however:
8571: 
8572: 1 The history of archaeological thinking runs in parallel with that in
8573: history in particular and the human sciences as a whole , though the
8574: jargon used is often different. I could have given similar potted
8575: histories of thought in sociology, economics, politics, etc.
8576: 
8577: 2 There are many different forms of historical thinking, many different
8578: theories of historical method. It is not sufficient, therefore, to say
8579: ‘we should reject history’ or indeed conversely ‘we should adopt tried
8580: and tested historical methods’ without specifying what those methods are
8581: and examining those methods within their own disciplinary context. While
8582: the theories of other disciplines can be a source of useful new ideas,
8583: there is therefore no salvation for archaeological thought in turning to
8584: the methods of another discipline, since the unity of methods professed
8585: by that discipline is invariably illusory whether we are discussing the
8586: methods of the natural sciences or those of history or of literary
8587: criticism.
8588: 
8589: Unsurprisingly, we find that different schools of archaeological theory
8590: have turned to different elements of current historical practice as
8591: allies.
8592: 
8593: Jacquetta Hawkes famously wrote of archaeology: ‘however scientific the
8594: methods employed, the final aims of the discipline remain historical:
8595: the description of individual events in time’ (Hawkes 1968: 258). Even
8596: when she wrote, this was a description of only one form of historical
8597: practice, and a narrowly traditional form at that. It was a form that
8598: Gordon Childe modelled his early culture-historical work on also. Childe
8599: in his later life came to question the theoretical assumptions of his
8600: earlier work and wrote critically of it in his ‘Retrospect’ (1958).
8601: 
8602: Recently, some archaeologists have turned to the ideas and methods of
8603: the Annales school for inspiration. In particular, they have stressed
8604: Annales interest in different timescales and its interplay of the
8605: material and the world of ‘mentalities’ in which no one feature is
8606: dominant. Finally, many postprocessualists derived inspiration from the
8607: methods and theories of historians involved in the New Cultural History.
8608: 
8609: Historical Archaeology
8610: 
8611: In the first part of this chapter, I looked at the relationship between
8612: history and archaeology in the abstract. I now want to look at how the
8613: two disciplines interact in practice, in different approaches to the
8614: archaeology of historic periods. My definition of ‘historical
8615: archaeology’ differs from that of many North American archaeologists,
8616: for whom this refers exclusively to the post-1500 period; I shall be
8617: discussing examples from Classical and medieval archaeology also.
8618: 
8619: Much traditional archaeology has found itself in alliance with
8620: traditional forms of history mentioned above. An earlier generation of
8621: archaeologists working in Classical periods or early medieval Europe
8622: deal with areas of historical discourse that, with exceptions, are still
8623: largely traditional in scope. In many cases, the archaeologists
8624: concerned took their first degree in Classics, History or a related
8625: discipline, and came to archaeology through a desire to attack questions
8626: defined in advance through (traditional) historical methods: Who was the
8627: king buried at Sutton Hoo? What was the appearance of the Forum in Rome?
8628: When was this medieval village deserted?
8629: 
8630: Subservience to historical accounts has meant subservience to
8631: traditional models of culture history and a normative view of culture.
8632: The classic example is the story of the Anglo-Saxon settlement of
8633: England. The eighth-century historian Bede tells us that three groups of
8634: settlers came to Britain in the fifth century ad: the Angles, the Saxons
8635: and the Jutes. Bede tells us where they settled and which kingdoms they
8636: founded. A generation of traditional archaeologists looked at the
8637: grave-goods from cemeteries in these areas and interpreted them by
8638: looking to north Germany and southern Denmark for parallels. Thus,
8639: because Bede tells us the county of Kent was settled by Jutes, material
8640: from Kent were labelled ‘Jutish’ and stylistic parallels sought – and
8641: found – with material from Jutland in Denmark of similar or slightly
8642: earlier date.
8643: 
8644: Though this is culture history based on theories of migration rather
8645: than diffusion, the parallels with prehistoric culture history are
8646: obvious. More recent work has criticized this model, either seeking to
8647: minimize the impact and numbers involved in fifth-century migrations, to
8648: be more critical about the historical record, or to stress social change
8649: at the level of small-scale structures rather than large-scale
8650: migrations.
8651: 
8652: Historical Archaeology and the Text
8653: 
8654: In North America in the 1960s and 1970s, a group of historical
8655: archaeologists worked to adapt the tenets of New Archaeology to the
8656: archaeological study of historic periods, following Binford’s insistence
8657: that ‘specialists in this field should provide the most informative
8658: tests or evaluations of ideas set forth by archaeologists in general’
8659: (1977: 169). We saw one example of such an approach in Hodges’s systemic
8660: analysis of Dark Age trade in chapter 5.
8661: 
8662: This work attempted to generalize and be anthropological in the sense of
8663: getting away from traditional historical descriptions of colonial and
8664: later history. It attempts to look for pattern – to define diagnostic
8665: patterns of artefacts; and tries to generalize about the societies under
8666: examination. For example, the work of Kathleen Deagan and others in St
8667: Augustine, Florida, a town populated in the seventeenth and eighteenth
8668: centuries by a number of different ethnic groups, has tried to look for
8669: different archaeological patterns associated with these groups. The aim
8670: here was in part to elaborate a more rigorous approach to the definition
8671: of ethnicity based on the definition of quantitative measures.
8672: 
8673: One way in which historical archaeology has taken up such concerns is to
8674: try to treat documents as a form of middle-range documentation. Remember
8675: that Binford argued that we could look to independent sources of
8676: information to build ‘robust’ arguments (chapter 4). In the
8677: Palaeolithic, he looked to animal and plant behaviour. Many have argued
8678: that in historic periods, documents can play a similar role. Account
8679: books at ports, for example, can tell us exactly what was being imported
8680: and exported and at what price, a pattern we can then match or fail to
8681: match against those goods or their containers when we find them in
8682: rubbish pits.
8683: 
8684: Conversely, many have turned to elements of postprocessual and
8685: interpretative thought for an alternative model of the integration of
8686: archaeology and history. Remember the importance of context in
8687: postprocessual thought. Documents read from this view can provide a
8688: special form of contextual information, for example on the individual or
8689: on different views of gender. We have already seen one example of this
8690: work: my discussion of the late medieval hall in England in chapter 7.
8691: 
8692: As a result, many explorations of recent theoretical trends have come in
8693: historical archaeology, particularly in colonial contexts – South Africa
8694: and North America especially. Two themes can be picked out in
8695: particular: first, the historical archaeology of capitalism; and second,
8696: identity in historical archaeology.
8697: 
8698: A historical archaeology of capitalism focuses on the interpretation of
8699: the period after 1500, and the way in which archaeology can address the
8700: most fundamental transition between medieval and modern worlds. This can
8701: be termed the ‘origins of capitalism’, the ‘feudal/capitalist
8702: transition’, ‘industrialization’ or the ‘origins of modernity’; each of
8703: these terms having different theoretical affiliations, and rather
8704: different nuances. A leading figure in the field is Mark Leone, who over
8705: the last 30 years has developed an account of the archaeology of
8706: capitalism in eighteenth- and nineteenth- century Annapolis and the
8707: Chesapeake, on the east coast of the United States. Leone and his
8708: students developed the idea of the ‘Georgian Order’, a material pattern
8709: first delineated by Henry Glassie and James Deetz. They saw principles
8710: of order, symmetry and segregation running through different classes of
8711: material culture – eighteenth-century architecture, gardens, pottery,
8712: landscape. The Georgian Order was interpreted by Leone and his students
8713: as linked to the emergence of merchant capitalism in the Chesapeake and
8714: beyond. Order, symmetry and segregation related to an ideology of the
8715: individual and of liberty, however much that ideology was contradicted
8716: by the realities of a class-divided and slave-owning eighteenth-century
8717: society.
8718: 
8719: Archaeologies of identity, discussed in chapter 8, have some of their
8720: most powerful case studies in historical archaeology, where different
8721: identities can be seen to emerge and be re-negotiated in both the
8722: archaeological and documentary records. Barbara Voss, for example, has
8723: shown how a distinctive ‘Californio’ identity of nineteenth-century
8724: Californians of Hispanic origin emerged as part of a whole complex of
8725: rapid cultural changes following the annexation of the area by the
8726: United States. ‘Archaeologies of resistance’ have looked for material
8727: evidence of identity formation, for example in institutional contexts
8728: such as prisons.
8729: 
8730: Historical archaeology as a whole, however, refuses to fit into the neat
8731: theoretical boxes that have very often been defined by prehistoric
8732: archaeologists. A good example of how historical archaeology simply does
8733: not ‘follow the rules’ is provided from the archaeology of castles.
8734: 
8735: Case Study: Bodiam Castle
8736: 
8737: There is a long-standing debate about castles in medieval England.
8738: Castles are high-status buildings, and combine accommodation for a great
8739: lord and his household with ‘defensive’ features – towers, ramparts,
8740: battlements, gatehouses, moats and ditches.
8741: 
8742: Interpretation of castles has traditionally been dominated by scholars
8743: with a military background. Developments in castle architecture have
8744: been interpreted in terms of changing techniques of attack and defence.
8745: This narrative often claims to be factual and atheoretical, but can
8746: easily be shown to be an evolutionary and progressive one: attack and
8747: defence become more technologically sophisticated over time. Both the
8748: evidence of archaeology (the changing form of walls, towers, gatehouses)
8749: and documents (narratives of sieges) have been used to tell a story
8750: about castles, quite literally: most traditional books on castles
8751: consist of a narrative history of the ‘rise and fall’ of the castle,
8752: with individual castles being cited as illustrative anecdotes.
8753: 
8754: In this story the documents take centre stage. By the very use of the
8755: word ‘castle’, rather than the more neutral and anthropological
8756: ‘high-status dwelling’, scholars use a word that is derived from the
8757: documents, and whose meanings are to some extent defined before
8758: archaeological analysis starts. Similar categories defined by the
8759: documents can be found across most of Classical and historical
8760: archaeology – ‘Roman villas’ (a ‘villa’ being defined with reference to
8761: Classical texts), ‘temples’, ‘churches’, the study of ‘enclosure’. Most
8762: narratives start with definitions of ‘castles’ based on the (usually
8763: Latin) texts within which words like ‘castrum’ and ‘castellum’ are
8764: defined. Next come the obviously military features. Residential features
8765: within the walls receive less attention, and the landscape context of
8766: castles – the villages and towns outside their walls – even less
8767: attention.
8768: 
8769: To over-simplify, these élite sites have generally been approached by
8770: archaeologists in ‘culture-historical’ ways, stressing influence,
8771: contact, diffusion of new architectural ideas. This document-based
8772: narrative is rarely questioned, let alone tested. Nothing like a
8773: ‘processual’ approach to castle studies has ever been proposed let alone
8774: pursued.
8775: 
8776: In recent years, some historians and archaeologists have asked other
8777: questions: what of the social dimensions of these buildings? Are not
8778: their lordly functions (judicial centres, lordly residences) at least as
8779: important as their supposed military/strategic role? Some have gone
8780: further still: what of the symbolic dimensions of these structures? Are
8781: the battlements and towers not in fact to do with military requirements,
8782: but rather the self-image of a masculine military élite basing its
8783: ideology on images of knighthood?
8784: 
8785: Bodiam Castle, near the Kent/Sussex border in south-east England
8786: (figures 11.1 and 11.2), was built in the 1380s by Sir Edward
8787: Dalyngrygge, an old soldier from the Hundred Year’s War in France. The
8788: interpretation of this castle has centred on the question: is this a
8789: seriously defensively strong castle, as the military theorists would
8790: have it? Or are its features better interpreted in a more complex
8791: symbolic and cultural way, as more recent scholars have argued?
8792: 
8793: Military theorists have drawn attention to the proliferation of military
8794: features: gunloops commanding the approach ways, twin towers flanking
8795: the gate and other towers dominating the tops of the walls. Most
8796: particularly, Bodiam is situated within a small rectangular lake that
8797: has been artificially created. The lake is a complex piece of hydraulic
8798: engineering, and is held back on two sides by an earthern dam. It
8799: apparently forms a formidable obstacle to any would-be attacker. The
8800: main entrance to Bodiam runs at an angle across this lake, exposing
8801: attackers to fire from the battlements.
8802: 
8803: Not only is Bodiam clearly militarily very strong on this analysis: the
8804: final ‘proof’ of the military thesis comes from a historical document, a
8805: ‘licence to crenellate [fortify]’. Translated from the Latin, it states
8806: apparently quite unequivocally that Sir Edward Dalyngrygge sought and
8807: duly obtained licence in October 1385 ‘to fortify with a wall of stone
8808: and lime the dwelling-place of the manor of Bodiam next to the sea … and
8809: to construct and make thereof a castle for the defence of the adjacent
8810: countryside and for the resisting of [the king’s] enemies’. Here is a
8811: clear, irrefutable documentary statement of the military purpose of the
8812: castle. Or is it?
8813: 
8814: Figure 11.1 Bodiam Castle: photograph of the north front and gatehouse
8815: across the lake
8816: 
8817: [c11_image001.jpg]
8818: 
8819: Disbelief in the exclusively military view of the castle has been
8820: relatively recent, and comes from three sources:
8821: 
8822: 1 A reassessment of the architecture of the castle. Charles Coulson has
8823: shown convincingly that many of the apparently ‘military’ features of
8824: the castle do not appear to ‘work’. Gunports are not placed effectively;
8825: guns placed in them do not command an effective field of fire. Other
8826: openings are almost impossible to use by longbow or crossbowmen; the
8827: fighting parapet is not easily accessible; the list could go on.
8828: 
8829: 2 A detailed survey, carried out independently, of the landscape context
8830: of the castle (figure 11.2). First, the earthwork dam is utterly
8831: undefended; in several estimations it could be cut through by a dozen
8832: workers in one night. The lake itself is not as deep as it could be; it
8833: is more a sheet of water like those of eighteenth-century ornamental
8834: gardens than a serious obstacle. Outside the lake are the earthworks of
8835: a large formal landscape, around which the movement of people and their
8836: views of the castle were carefully structured.
8837: 
8838: 3 A reassessment of the meaning of the licence to crenellate. Bodiam is
8839: not ‘next to the sea’; it is 14 kilometres north of the coast, at the
8840: end of a winding tidal estuary. Documents of this kind cannot be taken
8841: at face value as prehistorians might suppose. Coulson suggests that the
8842: production of such documents was largely part of a system of chivalric
8843: honour, and the references to defensive intent should not be read as an
8844: historical record, but rather as part of a knightly code of chivalrous
8845: values. He writes suggestively:
8846: 
8847: Reasoning from illusive defensive effects mechanistically back to
8848: imagined military causes has, in the case of Bodiam, led to the famous
8849: licence of October 1385 being taken at its face value. … Because no
8850: study has been done of licences to crenellate as a whole … many
8851: misjudgments have arisen partly from lack of contextual awareness .
8852: (Coulson 1992; emphasis mine)
8853: 
8854: Figure 11.2 Bodiam Castle: plan of landscape context
8855: 
8856: [c11_image002.jpg]
8857: 
8858: Coulson’s detailed knowledge and experience of late medieval documents,
8859: inductively acquired as part of his practice as a documentary historian,
8860: leads him to have a better ‘feel’ for the quality of the evidence than
8861: other writers on the subject.
8862: 
8863: But this reinterpretation is not simply a statement that ‘symbolic is
8864: more important than military’. Coulson writes:
8865: 
8866: The wisest course is to resist the temptation to write off any feature
8867: as ‘sham’, or to take any element as purely ‘functional’ (by which
8868: ‘defensive’ is intended normally). It is an artful combination which
8869: expresses … all the complex seignorial associations of the medieval
8870: castle-image, which included the deterring (and, if necessary, the
8871: defeating) of attack at whatever level was appropriate … the bravado of
8872: double portal chambers, ‘murder holes’ and three portcullises is mere
8873: rodomontade when the lateral doors are so weak. There is no ‘military’
8874: logic in trebling the main closures while leaving a short and direct
8875: approach to a weak back door (Postern) which entirely lacks elaboration.
8876: But there is a powerful psychological sense nonetheless: it is closer to
8877: Jean Froissart (and perhaps also to Franz Kafka) than it is to Vegetius
8878: or to the Sieur de Vauban. The fairy-tale element is here, allusive and
8879: romantic. … ‘Fortification’ was surely metaphysical as well as material;
8880: a matter of imagery and symbolism, not just of technology. (Coulson
8881: 1992: 66, 83)
8882: 
8883: What theoretical lessons can be drawn from the Bodiam story? First, the
8884: arguments are over formal rather than quantitative analysis. In other
8885: words, scholars argue not over the numbers of things, as they did over
8886: the relative proportions of different tool types in a given Mousterian
8887: assemblage as discussed in chapter 3, but over their form, whether that
8888: is of the form of the lake or of the gunloops. Such a way of debating
8889: derives in part from an existing archaeological tradition hitherto
8890: little discussed in this book. The so-called ‘English landscape
8891: tradition’ is a school of thought looking to the local historian W. G.
8892: Hoskins as its founder. The landscape tradition stresses close empirical
8893: analysis of pieces of landscape, a view of landscapes as both very old
8894: and as complex documents on which many phases of settlement are
8895: ‘written’, of the integration of history, archaeology and geography
8896: using an inductive model, and of hostility to ‘grand theory’. Such an
8897: approach has both strengths and weaknesses: it stresses
8898: interdisciplinarity and close attention to detail, but is essentially
8899: anecdotal in its approach. That is, it uses closely worked-through case
8900: studies but gives little consideration to how typical the case study or
8901: ‘anecdote’ is of the wider context. More seriously, it is almost never
8902: consciously theorized.
8903: 
8904: This debate, and the wider landscape tradition of which it is a part,
8905: does not follow the established rules of archaeological theory. While
8906: some approaches are more or less ‘culture-historical’ in nature, there
8907: is no established ‘processual’ view of Bodiam. No one has tried formally
8908: to characterize Bodiam in terms of a settlement hierarchy. No approach
8909: to the landscape around Bodiam that might approximate to a processual
8910: analysis has been taken. Archaeologists have not explicitly discussed
8911: English medieval society in terms of factional competition, religious
8912: legimitation, or access to resources, though the material for such an
8913: analysis is readily available and would make a compelling account.
8914: Conversely, Coulson’s discussion does not fit easily into theoretical
8915: categories: his discussion is peppered with aesthetic value judgements
8916: appropriate to the discourse of traditional architectural history but
8917: reading quite oddly to someone trained as a prehistoric archaeologist
8918: (Bodiam is ‘charmingly proportioned’, ‘elegant’, ‘nicely moulded’).
8919: Indeed, in my view Coulson’s arguments could be strengthened by
8920: reference to such theory, considering for example which social groups
8921: and/or genders would have ‘read aright’ the symbolism of Bodiam, and
8922: considering in more depth the peasant-landlord antagonism of the period.
8923: 
8924: Conclusion
8925: 
8926: Archaeological theory has tended to be played out over certain classic
8927: ‘case studies’ (I almost flippantly wrote ‘playgrounds’) such as
8928: megaliths, cave art, the Mousterian question. These have tended to be
8929: prehistoric, for several reasons. First, prehistory is perceived as
8930: ‘pure’ archaeology. Second, most histories of archaeology have seen
8931: historic periods as secondary to the main story of the development of
8932: prehistoric chronology. In passing, it is worth noting that such
8933: histories themselves need rewriting; for example, nineteenth-century
8934: European medievalists were well aware of stratigraphy in buildings
8935: archaeology, and documented such stratigraphy carefully, independently
8936: of more well-known prehistoric studies of excavated stratigraphy. Third,
8937: the stress on very long-term processes within New Archaeology tended to
8938: make the shorter-term perspectives of historical archaeologists marginal
8939: to the discipline.
8940: 
8941: The story of historic archaeology shows that we do not need to stick to
8942: the notion of prehistory as ‘pure’ archaeology, and that the archaeology
8943: of historic periods throws up equally relevant and complex problems of
8944: interpretation. In addition, the cross-cutting influence of different
8945: disciplines (archaeology, history, literature, etc.) throws up a series
8946: of unexpected theoretical debates that enrich archaeological discourse
8947: as a whole. If you want to find the most informed and up-to-date studies
8948: of gender and other aspects of identity, to examine the cultural and
8949: political engagement of archaeology, or to explore themes like agency
8950: and materiality, then it is increasingly the case that the most exciting
8951: and compelling studies come from historical archaeology. In the last 20
8952: years, historical archaeology has moved from the margins of the
8953: discipline to centre stage.
8954: 
8955: 12
8956:
